issue_title: Use translator-friendlier names on CrowdIn
issue_body:
Right now we name our frontend and backend strings as `LC_FRONTEND_MESSAGES` and `LC_MESSAGES` in CrowdIn, respectively. These names look intimidating, and there's really no reason for translators to know this specific implementation detail.

We should rename these folders to `frontend` and `backend`, respectively. It should be easy to change it here: https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/develop/crowdin.yaml#L6-L13

By just changing the `translation` key in that file, we can change the way these folders appear on CrowdIn, while allowing us to still be compatible with what Django and our build system expects.

I'm trying to build a variable to pass to the REST API. What I've found is that when the variable is is passed to XMLDictNode and emit_xml is called, things get re-ordered and this causes the rest api to reject the request. If I create the config var by parsing an XML string, the reordering does not happen. I even used the output from print to configure the variable, but the reordering still happens when the variable is set directly. I've included a small test program that exhibits this behaviour. Any idea why this happens or how to avoid it?

```

"""

import email
import getpass
import pprint

import requests
import jxmlease


# Create a jxmlease parser with desired defaults.

    """The main loop.
    """
    # parsing XML works
    root = jxmlease.parse("""<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<configuration>

    print('----    ----')
    return config_node

if __name__ == "__main__":
```

issue_body:
We have some asterix recordings in final format with a missing byte from time to time.

Running `asterix  -d /etc/asterix/asterix.ini -F -f missing_byte.ff -j` gives:

```
{"timestamp":4244580,"CAT002":{"I010":{"SAC":64,"SIC":11},"I000":{"type":2},"I020":{"Azi":112.5000000},"I030":{"ToD":4243.6093750}}},
ERROR:   Sep 16 09:21:56 2015 (asterixformat.cxx) Wrong Asterix data length (11)
ERROR:   Sep 16 09:21:56 2015 (asterixformat.cxx) DataItem::parse needed length=3 , and there is only 2

terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::logic_error'
  what():  basic_string::_S_construct null not valid
Aborted (core dumped)
```

Indead, using recview, it's easy to see that one byte is missing at 01:10:44,76:

issue_title: base layer hooks hardcode Python2
issue_body:
The install, upgrade-charm, start, stop etc. hooks in the reactive basic layer all use '#!/bin/env python' as the shebang line, which is Python2. Rather than force a version of Python that has been deprecated for several years and will soon stop being on the cloud images by default, 'charm build' should default to generating Python3 stubs and perhaps provide an option to use Python2 when compatibility is needed with legacy code.

layer.yaml could be used to declare if Python2 compatibility is needed.

If both Python2 and Python3 are supported, 'charm build' will need to detect and correct shebang lines somehow. The edge case here being a Python3 charm that includes copies of legacy Python2 scripts (eg. administrative scripts to be invoked from cron).

issue_title: Docker from within docker
issue_body:
When running girder_worker inside of a docker container, running docker tasks can be problematic.   

For reference, we have three components we need to refer to: (1) host - the host machine, (2) worker docker - girder_worker running in a docker container, and (3) task docker - the task we want to run in a docker container via the girder_worker docker plugin.

There are two basic approaches to run a docker container from within another docker container.

Approach 1: run the task docker on the host, which can be done by mounting two volumes on the worker docker: `-v /usr/bin/docker:/usr/bin/docker -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (assuming host and worker_docker are appropriate flavors of linux).

Approach 2: https://github.com/jpetazzo/dind , where a bash script does a bunch of magic as part of the start up of the task docker.

I don't like approach 2.  We already run a script in the task docker which assumes that the task docker is debian-like and has bash.  This doesn't work right in alpine or BusyBox where there are no `groupadd` and `useradd` commands.  This would further limit what can be run as a task.

In Approach 1, the task docker can run *but* the mount points for the temporary directory and scripts directory end up referring to locations on the *host*, but were intended to refer to locations on the *worker docker*.  A crude work around for this would be to copy our script to the temp directory (requiring a change in the docker plugin) and to set the `tmp_root` to a path that is identical on the host and the worker docker and is volume mounted between the two.

I'm not sure if Approach 2 would work easily, as I haven't actually tried it out.

Any other recommendations on how to accomplish running docker tasks in a docker worker?

issue_title: Make `docker` a namespace package
issue_body:
Currently it's not possible for other projects to define their own modules under the `docker` namespace. As the core of docker is simplified (e.g. removing `images --tree`) you can imagine plenty of projects popping up that extend the functionality of docker. It's logical for these projects to use modules like `docker.treeviz` or some such. For this to work, docker-py needs to declare `docker` a namespace package.

See https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.schema/blob/master/setup.py#L93

issue_title: Wrong documentation of the parameter "networks" of containers.run() and containers.create()
issue_body: The documentation says that the parameter "networks" is supported and a list of network names should be passed. But the parameter is called "network" and only a single network can be passed to both commands. An update of the documentation or the commands is needed.

issue_title: Build() should default to `pull=False`
issue_body:
The default behavior of docker cli when building does not pull by default. You must provide the `--pull` flag. I suggest that `docker-py` follows the same rule and default to `pull=False`

https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/docker/client.py#L309

issue_title: Requests v2.11.0 causes breakage
issue_body:
This is a known issue within requests and looks like it will be fixed in v2.11.1. Documenting for the benefit of anyone else who runs into this :).

After a recent pip upgrade I found that docker-py was passing along an error from requests:

File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/docker/client.py", line 307, in _stream_raw_result
   for out in response.iter_content(chunk_size=1, decode_unicode=True):
 File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/requests/utils.py", line 372, in stream_decode_response_unicode
   raise UnicodeError("Unable to decode contents with encoding %s." % encoding)
UnicodeError: Unable to decode contents with encoding None.

This has already been reported to requests (https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/3481) and fixed (https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/commit/d7f56ba9383575a6b7d361db0123a93c70a2b42f) for the next version.

If you are running into this issue, the easiest fix for now appears to be reverting to a pre 2.11 version of requests.

issue_title: Logging changes in 55ca3e40ab create problems with older code, e.g. autobahn-python
issue_body:
Crux of the issue seem to be that e.g. autobahn-python does things like these:

    self.log.debug("received HTTP headers in opening handshake : %s" % str(self.http_headers))

And new logging api (introduced in 55ca3e40ab, according to git-blame) does not support that, as it then calls `str.format()` on the passed string, which should never be called on essentially random string like one produced by the line above.

Link to that line in current autobahn-python: https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/c6ca54d794/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py#L2327

Submitted issue here, as fixing logging calls in autobahn should leave-out anyone with older autobahn and newer txaio, so thought that maybe it's possible to have fix here, making old logging calls compatible with new api instead?

A bit more details follow.

------

So, when using WAMP in autobahn-python with Python-3.5/asyncio, I'm seeing this:

```
2016-04-04 20:39:12 :: asyncio ERROR: Exception in callback WebSocketAdapterProtocol._consume.<locals>.process(<Future finished result=None>) at /home/fraggod/.py3/autobahn/asyncio/websocket.py:105
handle: <Handle WebSocketAdapterProtocol._consume.<locals>.process(<Future finished result=None>) at /home/fraggod/.py3/autobahn/asyncio/websocket.py:105>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/asyncio/events.py", line 125, in _run
    self._callback(*self._args)
  File "/home/fraggod/.py3/autobahn/asyncio/websocket.py", line 109, in process
    self._dataReceived(data)
  File "/home/fraggod/.py3/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py", line 1176, in _dataReceived
    self.consumeData()
  File "/home/fraggod/.py3/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py", line 1205, in consumeData
    self.processHandshake()
  File "/home/fraggod/.py3/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py", line 3484, in processHandshake
    self.log.debug("received HTTP headers in opening handshake : %s" % str(self.http_headers))
  File "/home/fraggod/.py3/txaio/aio.py", line 109, in _log
    msg = log_format.format(**kwargs)
KeyError: "'sec-websocket-protocol'"
2016-04-04T20:39:12 Exception in callback WebSocketAdapterProtocol._consume.<locals>.process(<Future finished result=None>) at /home/fraggod/.py3/autobahn/asyncio/websocket.py:105
handle: <Handle WebSocketAdapterProtocol._consume.<locals>.process(<Future finished result=None>) at /home/fraggod/.py3/autobahn/asyncio/websocket.py:105>
```

Looking into txaio.aio, issue seem to be that `msg = log_format.format(**kwargs)` is with "log_format" already having templated-in data - in this case headers dict - and hence looks like this:

    received HTTP headers in opening handshake : {'sec-websocket-protocol': 'wamp.2.json.batched', ...

And then running pretty much any `str.format()` on that would inevitably produce error above, due to `{}` things in there.

issue_title: deis-store-volume stuck in a restart loop
issue_body:
We have seen this happen occasionally, and there are two causes of this:

* The version of CoreOS is too old and doesn't have the Ceph FS kernel driver
 * Deis requires at least CoreOS 472.0.0 for the Ceph FS kernel driver and Docker 1.3.0 (although the newer the release, the better - >= 490.0.0 is recommended)
* One or more of the `store-daemon` containers is not running
 * This can be caused by etcd/fleet issues on the host (which we've seen when the machine is under heavy load), but it also appears that sometimes the daemons stall out - see #2520
 * The daemons can be restarted with `deisctl restart store-daemon`.

For more details on troubleshooting deis-store components, see [Troubleshooting Deis](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/troubleshooting_deis/#a-deis-store-component-fails-to-start).

issue_title: Add some way of protecting the refresh token / not storing it as plaintext?
issue_body:
I was asked about this at GlobusWorld, and, while we probably don't want to tackle this today, we should record the request/thought. There are several variants of this.

One option is for us to say that "You should encrypt your homedir, or link `~/.globus.cfg` to a file in an encrypted dir". That works today, but it presumes

- that the user is comfortable working with dir encryption of some kind
- that the user is okay with the refresh token being protected, for the case of homedir encryption, only from attackers who do not have any ability to act as the user (vs. a malicious program)

In terms of actual solutions, there are many variants, but some options as food for thought:

- password-protect using `globus lock` and `globus unlock` commands
- allow the user to set or pass (to any command) an encryption keyfile, which is used to encrypt/decrypt auth data
  Users are free to manage the keyfile however they like
- allow `globus login --ephemeral` which _does not_ request refresh tokens, and says its for short-term use only
- let the user provide (command-line or config) a `secret_decrypt_hook` and `secret_encrypt_hook` which is a command to run in subprocess; perhaps with special value "openssl" which we know means to "do the right thing" with the openssl commandline tool and a password

issue_title: trusted endpoint organization
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is the right board to post on, but here goes! I'm interested in what would be a good way to go about making an endpoint "trusted" in terms of it's organization. This means that, if I have endpoint A and call it a "sregistry endpoint" I can be guaranteed to file a particular kind of organization and type of file. I can give it some configuration to determine the structure, and if I (or anyone else) try to do an operation that goes against the expected structure I am not allowed. I can then associate this predictability with a label for others to find. Even better, I can also set constraints like this for metadata, so I can be sure that these endpoints provide something that looks like an API for associated metadata (and I think worst case some json file named equivalently to the image could be stored alongside each image for reading it, albeit it's not queryable). The use case looks like this:

1. I create a local registry (an organized storage folder) of Singularity images. This means that an organization is expected of collection folders with images named based on URIs, and that no files other than Singularity will be found in there.
2. I register my endpoint for some discovery tool so other uses can easily find it. I don't give anyone write permission, but I give global read permission so anyone can grab my images.
3. Then when I am interested in getting images from others, I use the discovery endpoint to search and filter down to some image I'm interested in. I do a "pull" sort of operation and get the image into my space locally, **or** just save the endpoint where I found it and other metadata for later usage.

issue_title: Add `total_latency_ms` and `queue_length` metric for Unity LUN.
issue_body:
Add Unity LUN metrics `total_latency_ms` and `queue_length`.

Related paths:
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.totalIoTime
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.currentIOCount
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.busyTime
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.idleTime
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.reads
* sp.\*.storage.lun.\*.writes

Formula:
* Total latency (ms):
>  ((spa totalIoTime) + (spb totalTime)) / ((spa total calls) + (spb total calls))/1000
* Average Queue Length for each SP (count):
>  delta(currentIOCount) * (delta(busyTime)/(delta(idleTime) + delta(busyTime)))
* Average Queue Length (count): 
> ((spa Queue Length) * (spa total calls) + (spb Queue Length) * (spb total calls)) / ((spa total calls) + (spb total calls))

issue_title: p2p and sub-proto handshakes cannot be cancelled externally
issue_body:
The `peer.handshake()` coroutine gets passed a `CancelToken` so that it can be cancelled, but that token is only used during the auth handshake (it gets passed to the `auth.handshake()` coroutine), and not during the p2p/sub-proto handshakes as those are implemented as methods on the `Peer` class and there we use the instance's own `CancelToken`. The problem is that the instance's token cannot be triggered externally until after the `peer.handshake()` coroutine has returned the instance itself (i.e. after the p2p/sub-proto handshakes).

We could change the `Peer` constructor to take an optional `CancelToken` and chain that with a new one to obtain its own, but that would cause the `Peer` instance to finish when the original `CancelToken` is triggered. I think that makes sense when the original token comes from a `PeerPool`, but will have to double check that this won't have unintended consequences in other cases

issue_title: Add lightchain integration test to CI
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The lightchain integration test is not currently running in CI, so it breaks regularly, and for extended periods.

(eg~ broken from #869 to #913)

### How can it be fixed

Instructions for a manual local run are here: https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/913/files#diff-19d5ae6516fbe4a30f0bcc2f45e8dc00R36

Run it locally first to be sure that tests pass on your master.

One time prep as described in the comment in the code linked above:
- Set up geth with a >=11 blocks of `geth --testnet --syncmode full` (should happen essentially instantly after you find a peer). Try to run it as short as possible while still having the first 11 blocks
- Note the `enode` in the geth logs
- tar up the geth data dir (it should be very small with so few blocks)
- add the geth data dir to a new file called `tests/p2p/integration/geth-data-ropsten.tar.gz`
- move the lightchain integration test from `tests/p2p/test_lightchain_integration.py` to `tests/p2p/integration/test_lightchain.py`

Add a new circle job that runs just that integration test. In that job only:
- untar the geth data dir
- install geth, and cache it (see web3.py integration tests with geth for some examples)
- run geth with discovery off: `geth --testnet --lightserv 90 --nodiscover`
- run only this integration test `pytest --integration --capture=no --enode="enode://$ENODE_PUBKEY_FROM_EARLIER@127.0.0.1:30303" tests/p2p/integration/test_lightchain.py`

issue_title: -lpython linked against wrong python in python extensions on osx
issue_body:
When building fortran extensions with conda under osx I need to add the this line my build.sh

```
if [ `uname` == Darwin ]; then export LDFLAGS="-lpython" ; fi
```

Unfortunately the object is then linked against the Mac's system python:

Leading to a runtime error:

```
import: u'ort'
Fatal Python error: PyThreadState_Get: no current thread
```

A quick look shows bad linkage to the system's Python:

```
otool -L lib/python2.7/site-packages/ort.so
lib/python2.7/site-packages/ort.so:
    /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python (compatibility version 2.7.0, current version 2.7.10)
    @rpath/libgfortran.3.dylib (compatibility version 4.0.0, current version 4.0.0)
    /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1226.10.1)
    @rpath/libgcc_s.1.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1.0.0)
    @rpath/libquadmath.0.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1.0.0)
```

As a result I need to manually relink in my build.sh by using:

```
if [ `uname` == Darwin ]; then install_name_tool -change /System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/Python @rpath/libpython2.7.dylib ${SP_DIR}/*.so ; fi
```

I feel this last step could probably be handled automagically by `conda` itself.

issue_title: python package should require pip
issue_body:
I don't think it should be possible to get a conda environment with Python and without pip. This is a source of confusion, where people set up an env, pip install a few things, and realize (or don't) that pip is still associated with the system Python.

It is increasingly rare these days to have any Python installation without pip. It should be a safe assumption that if you have Python in an environment, pip is there, too.

issue_title: Disable .netrc authentication
issue_body:
Anaconda-Server/support#23

When a user has a `~/.netrc` file present, `requests` will use that file to populate the `Authorization` header (using HTTP Basic auth). When authorization fails, conda falls into an infinite loop retrying.

To fix this problem in `anaconda-client`, I disabled `.netrc` authorization by adding a `NullAuth` to the `requests.Session`.

https://github.com/Anaconda-Server/anaconda-client/pull/298

issue_title: Install without tk
issue_body:
I want to be able to create a conda environment, specifically a python environment, without tk - that's a dependency I don't need (and is unnecessary when my code will eventually run on a headless server). However, if I try `conda remove tk` it also removes `pip, python, setuptools, wheel`. And if I then try to re-install, say, `pip`, it forces me to reinstall `tk` again. I don't need or want GUI support.

Any way around this?

Thanks

issue_title: Interpolation of integers
issue_body:
Assume this config:

```
# config
a = 1
b = 2
c = "$a + $a = $b"
# spec
a = integer
b = integer
c = string
```

It finds that `c` matches with Template regex, tries to substitute `a` into `c`, finds `a` and tries to match Template regex with `a` that is integer because of spec applied. So, it raises `TypeError: expected string or bytes-like object`.

issue_title: Feature request: make index file parameterisable for VariantFile (and others?)
issue_body:
Currently, when we open a VariantFile from a HTTP or FTP URL, the low-level htslib code checks for the existence of the corresponding index file, and writes it to the current working directory, if it exists. This behaviour is perfectly fine for many applications, but is not suitable when many different files may be opened at the same time, which may happen to have the same index names. It is also not very good for the GA4GH server, where we want to allow HTTP URLs to be allowed as a data source (see https://github.com/ga4gh/server/pull/232 for details). We don't want our server blocking for an arbitrary amount of time while it downloads index files, and also having an apache process writing lots of essential files (possibly in parallel, overwriting and corrupting partially downloaded files, since there will be several processes) to the file system.

It would be much better if we could pre-download the index files, and then provide a base URL from which we can find the data file. This should be straightforward enough to do, but we'd need to have the option of specifying the index file to VariantFile when we open the URL (and others; presumably, we'll want to do the same thing for SAM files too).

Is this feasible? I don't mind doing the legwork if you give me some pointers on where to start.

issue_title: Azure WebApp config container set should not require creds for ACR repos
issue_body: When configuring an Azure WebApp to run a container, the registry URI, password, and login/user are required. This pretty cumbersome when working with ACR, since you need to write several commands to fetch the credential values. Instead, the command should accept an image name and (optional) resource ID or name for an ACR repo.

issue_title: [Interactive] Unable to debug in Interactive mode
issue_body:
1. I ran `az interactive --debug` to get debugging output for each of my calls. However, when I do that, any kind of dynamic tab completion (which occurs on basically every command because of resource group) causes the screen to be overrun with tons of debugging text related to the tab completion REST API calls. Thus you can't even see what you were typing. There doesn't seem to be a way to turn this off.

2. I can't even run select calls with --debug. This just does nothing at all and prints normal output.

issue_title: API objects can't make round trip
issue_body:
This may not be true for all objects retrieved via the various `get()` methods, but take attaching a data disk to a `VirtualMachine` instance for example. To attach a data disk to a VM, the process seems like it should be:

1. get the VM from the API
` vm = compute_client.virtual_machines.get(resource_group_name, vm_name)`

2. modify the local instance by appending a data disk to the VM
`vm.storage_profile.data_disks.append(data_disk)`

3. update the VM
`compute_client.virtual_machines.create_or_update(resource_group_name, vm)`

However, the following error is generated from API when attempting the above:

```
'{"error":{"code":"InvalidRequestContent","message":"The request content was invalid and could not be deserialized: \\'Could not find member \\'resources\\' on object of type \\'ResourceDefinition\\'. Path \\'resources\\', line 1, position 1507.\\'."}}'
```

This appears to be because the `extensions` property of the VM instance retrieved from the API generates an empty list, rather than a `None` value. When calling `create_or_update()`, the empty list starts to cause problems here:

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/azure-mgmt-compute/azure/mgmt/compute/computemanagement.py#L6697

Ultimately this is where the `resources` object is set (and is what the API is complaining when the request is submitted):

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/azure-mgmt-compute/azure/mgmt/compute/computemanagement.py#L6805

So it seems like either a) the Python SDK  should treat empty lists (and maybe dictionaries?) the same as a `None` property value or b) the ARM API should handle this case more gracefully.

Obviously the workaround for this particular example is `vm.extensions = None` between the calls to `get()` and `create_or_update()`, but that seems like it shouldn't be necessary.

issue_title: Python MultiDict constructor discards generator content
issue_body:
When using the pure Python implementation, if a `MultiDict` is constructed with a generator argument:

    headers = CIMultiDict(
        (
            k.decode('utf-8', 'surrogateescape'),
            v.decode('utf-8', 'surrogateescape'),
        )
        for k, v in event.headers
    )

then the resulting `MultiDict` will be empty, instead of containing the key/value pairs as expected. This is because the generator is iterated over twice, and the first iteration discards all of the pairs.

issue_title: Artifact in LaTeX/MathJax rendering
issue_body:
I'm getting the following artifact of a right sided border on rendered MathJax objects (see image) from either manually created LaTeX code (as below) or Sympy expression renderings. The issue happens for both $ ... $ and $$ ... $$ items, where the MathJax object terminates; inline expressions will have them, too. This was working on the default install of a plain Python installation, using notebook 4.1, but under the most recent distribution of Anaconda, it's happening.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/197778/12616595/0625ccb8-c4da-11e5-9c58-3f1e90dabf36.png)

```
We know the following:
* Side AC = 500
* Side BC = 300
* Angle BAC = 36.87
* Angle ABC = 90

We need to solve for side AB. As described, angle BAC allows us to use its cosine ratio to derive AB, since it is adjacent and side AC is opposite angle ABC, and thus is the hypotenuse:

$\cos{36.87} = \frac{AB}{500}$  
$500 \cdot \cos{36.87} = AB$  
$400 = AB$

We can check this using the Pythagorean theorem, $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$:  
$300^2+b^2=500^2$  
$90000+b^2=250000$  
$b^2 = 160000$  
$b = \sqrt{160000} = 400$
```

I can do further testing, but wanted to at least bring this to your attention first to know if I'm missing something here. Thanks!

issue_title: [request] reconsider using xdg paths
issue_body:
https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/48
https://github.com/ipython/ipython/pull/4457
https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/6070
https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/211

Sorry to beat this apparently dead horse, but I was testing out Jupyter and just realised that the config directory was wrong. And then with growing disbelief discovered that this was once reverted and even held onto.

The "often used in documentation" issue is cleanly solved by calling it [config dir] and linking to a page containing the paths listed by OS. This is standard procedure, don't tell me nobody with documentation commit access wants to take the time to create a single additional page and run `find doc/ -name "*.html" | xargs sed -i 's/\.jupyter/\<a href...\>\[config dir\]\<\/a\>/'"` (obviously run the adequate link syntax on the source of however you build your doc, possible mistakes should be trivially checkable with grep).

And unless the config directory on Windows is ~/.jupyter as well (haven't checked, but assume/hope it's not), this weird "config goes here" idea could be strictly improved by hard-coding it to Desktop/jupyter-config (or Desktop\jupyter-config, if you will) to be consistent across platforms. Then you don't have to ask users about their platform (or point them to the doc page) AND don't have to tell them to navigate a terminal / unhide files (which is platform specific).

On a more serious note, going by the disussion so far there really seem to be no valid reasons to hold onto ~/.jupyter (`jupyter --paths` tells me config/data/runtime are separated nowadays), whereas OSX and Linux users would be thankful for the proper locations, as would those who have to deploy Jupyter in computer rooms... so here's another plea to reconsider a little bit before wontfixing this ;)

issue_title: Proposal for enhancing display()
issue_body:
Since `display()` is becoming an IPython primitive (being automatically injected into builtins) it would be nice to make display do a little bit more. Two ideas:

First, it would be nice if `display` also did the work of `clear_output()`, and `clear_output(wait=True)`. For example, making it work such that:

```python
def display(*objs, clear_output=True, wait=True, **kwargs):
    if clear_output:
        IPython.display.clear_output(wait=wait)
    ...
```
`metakernel` has display that works this way and it is quite useful.

Second, it would be nice to add a `sep` argument (similar to `print`)so that we can display items on the same line:

```python
display(item1, item2, sep=" ")
```

`display` currently puts each display in its own `span`, so the sep might have to be a keyword rather than arbitrary HTML to allow items to appear on the same line. 

There may be other functionalities that would be nice to include in display(), but these two I run into often.

issue_title: Validation
issue_body:
There are a number of open issues regarding model validation. Unfortunately, it's an aspect of Schematics that's perhaps the most difficult to work on because the current system is somewhat confusing.

In addition to the issues already reported, there are a few details I'd like to ask about:

* Validating a model will reset any empty fields to their default values:

  ```python
  class M(Model):
      n = IntType(default=123)
  ```
  ```python
  >>> m = M()
  >>> m.n = None
  >>> m.to_primitive()
  {'n': None}
  >>> m.validate()
  >>> m.to_primitive()
  {'n': 123}
  ```

  Bug or not?

  (The first version of the Undefined feature introduced a new parameter called `apply_defaults`, which can be used to control this when importing and validating. However, the appropriate default setting for validation depends on whether the current behavior is by design or accident.)

* By the time `_check_for_unknown_fields()` is called from the main `validate()` function, `import_loop` will have already checked for rogue fields.

  What is then the purpose of `_check_for_unknown_fields()`?

* If a type-level validator fails, a model-level validator for the same field will not be called, as the field will have been dropped from `data` by then.

  Bug or not?

issue_title: Using autograd
issue_body:
I'm trying to find coefficients of a function f(y) and the w such that d^2y/dt^2 + w^2y = f(y) matches some given data set. To do this, I'm using scipy's odeint and gradient descent, with the following code

f = open('foo', 'r')
t = f.readline().rstrip().split()
real = f.readline().rstrip().split()
real = list(map(float,real))
t = list(map(float,t))
#t =  t = np.linspace(0, 50, 500)

def objective(params, step):
    sol = odeint(osc, q0, t, args=(params[0],params[1],params[2], params[3]), full_output = 0)
    real = np.array(real)
    error = np.sum((sol[:,0]-real)**2)
    return error
def osc(q, t, w, coef1,coef2,coef3):
    f = np.poly1d([coef1,coef2,coef3])
    x, y = q
    dqdt = [y, -w**2*x +f(x)]
    return dqdt
w = 1.0
coef1 = 0.0
coef2 = 0.0
coef3 = 0.0

params =(w,coef1,coef2,coef3)
q0 = [2,1]

def callback(params, step, g):
    if step % 1000 == 0:
        print("Iteration {0:3d} objective {1}".format(step, objective(params, step)))
        
print(type(grad(objective)))
params = adam(elementwise_grad(objective), params, step_size=0.001, num_iters=5001, callback=callback)        

However, the type passed into objective by autograd (ArrayBox) seems to violently disagree with odeint. Is there anything I can do to assuage this, or is there another way I could approach the problem?

issue_title: mulled should not use continuumio/miniconda3:latest tag
issue_body:
`mulled` is too susceptible to unforeseen changes in the `continuumio/miniconda3` images by using the `latest` tag. See https://github.com/bioconda/bioconda-recipes/pull/8044#issuecomment-369404165:
> The build failure is due to `mulled` pulling `continuumio/miniconda3:latest` which, unfortunately, has been updated to `4.4.10`: https://hub.docker.com/r/continuumio/miniconda/tags/. That caused the `mulled` build to run into https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6811

issue_title: How to use `override`?
issue_body:
In the discussion of #186 I learned that `peru override` is the way to go when you want to develop an included ('guest') project from an including ('host') project; seen from the file layout, this would look like

```
guest-repo/
  .git/
  somefile
  ...
host/
  peru.yaml
  guest-copy/
    somefile
    ...
  ...
```

The readme only states that `override` "Replace[s] the contents of a module with a local directory path, usually a clone you've made of the same repo. This lets you test changes to imported modules without needing to push your changes upstream or edit `peru.yaml`." which leaves me puzzled how to organize my workflow. 

I'd greatly appreciate any help here because what I'm doing now is editing files in my local copy (`host/guest-copy/somefile`), seeing that the changed guest code works in the intended habitat, and then use `meld` to shuffle changes back to the guest repo (`guest-repo/somefile`). Clearly, that's less than ideal.

issue_title: Screen/minimap position inconsistencies
issue_body:
I'm using a screen size of (64,64), a minimap size of (64,64), and camera of size 24 on map Simple64. This results in a camera on the minimap of size (16,16). 

I'd expect that a 1 pixel shift on the minimap results in a 4 pixel shift across the map since 64/16 = 4. This seems to be true, except every once in a while the pixel shift is only 2 resulting in inconsistent amount of traversal across the real map for traversal across the minimap. 

I'm guessing this might have to do with the map having a size that isn't a perfect factor of 64? Any thoughts on why this might be happening?

Code that reproduces the issue:
```
from PIL import Image

from pysc2.agents import base_agent
from pysc2.lib import actions
from pysc2.env.environment import StepType

class Prototype(base_agent.BaseAgent):

    def __init__(self):
        super(Prototype, self).__init__()
        self.action_queue = [
                              # 2 px shift
#                             actions.FunctionCall(1, [(45, 45)]),
#                             actions.FunctionCall(1, [(46, 45)])

                             # Expected 4 px shift
                             actions.FunctionCall(1, [(45, 45)]),
                             actions.FunctionCall(1, [(44, 45)])
                            ]
        self.ix = list(range(len(self.action_queue)))
    
    def step(self, obs):
        super(Prototype, self).step(obs)
        
        if obs.step_type != StepType.FIRST:
            minimap = obs.observation['minimap']
            screen = obs.observation['screen']
        
            ix = self.ix.pop(0)
            
            Image.fromarray((screen[4] > 0).astype('uint8')*255).save('~/{}_screen.png'.format(ix))
            Image.fromarray((minimap[3] > 0).astype('uint8')*255).save('~/{}_minimap.png'.format(ix))
        
        return self.action_queue.pop(0)
```

`python -m pysc2.bin.agent --agent agents.debugging_1.Prototype --map Simple64 --screen_resolution 64 --agent_race T`

issue_title: How to debug a reactive charm
issue_body:
Stolen from [Ask Ubuntu](https://askubuntu.com/questions/804465/juju-charms-how-to-run-and-debug-reactive-charm):

> I'm developing a juju charm in python using a reactive pattern. All my hooks are called using decorators provided by 'basic' base layer. When trying to deploy the charm I encountered an error. In order to solve the error I used 'juju debug-hooks' and 'juju dhx -r' (dhx debugging plugin) but I can't find a way to run charm code using python following reactive pattern... As I understand, (when using dhx for debugging for example) the charm execution is returned to the state exactly before the hook that caused an error and the developer is logged in into a remote debugging session. It is on the developer to run hooks. Normally these hooks would be under /hooks directory and would be run by just executing them. This isn't possible using reactive pattern, since there is no entry point for hooks but everything is just a cascade of reactions to states...

I think this question raises two good points:
1. We should maintain a section for frequently asked questions that can occasionally be updated based on feedback on Ask Ubuntu, #juju, or the mailing lists.
2. In a case of this question, we should link to the relevant Juju documentation about debugging a charm in general, and then talk about the specific case of debugging a reactive charm.

issue_title: 0.5.0.b0: charms.reactive.bus.get_state is missing (charms.openstack)
issue_body:
As part of the testing of of the 0.5.0.b0 tagged release, barbican (which is built with `charms.openstack`) has the following error:

```
2017-09-18 14:32:01 DEBUG install     if not self.get_state('haproxy.stat.password'):
2017-09-18 14:32:01 DEBUG install   File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/charms_openstack/charm/core.py", line 348, in get_state
2017-09-18 14:32:01 DEBUG install     return reactive.bus.get_state(state)
2017-09-18 14:32:01 DEBUG install AttributeError: module 'charms.reactive.bus' has no attribute 'get_state'
```

In `charms.openstack` the get_state, set_state and remove_state are used as:

```python
    def set_state(self, state, value=None):
        """proxy for charms.reactive.bus.set_state()"""
        reactive.bus.set_state(state, value)

    def remove_state(self, state):
        """proxy for charms.reactive.bus.remove_state()"""
        reactive.bus.remove_state(state)

    def get_state(self, state):
        """proxy for charms.reactive.bus.get_state()"""
        return reactive.bus.get_state(state)
```

These tend to be used quite heavily in `charms.openstack` derived charms.  One way to fix it (on the `charms.openstack` side) would be to just:

```python
    def set_state(self, state, value=None):
        """proxy for charms.reactive.bus.set_state()"""
        try:
            reactive.bus.set_state(state, value)
        except AttributeError:
            ...set_flag(state, value)
```

... and also adding `set_flag(...)` *et al.* to `charms.openstack` and adding deprecations to the `set_state(...)` *et al.* functions.

Not sure on the best approach here.

issue_title: Expose bind and ingress addresses on Endpoint & Relation
issue_body:
charms.reactive.Endpoint should expose network-get results, providing the bind and ingress addresses for the endpoint. It should also be exposed on the Relation in the same way.

For the 'cluster' endpoint, we get data that looks like this:
```$ juju run --unit=dse/1 -- network-get cluster --format=yaml```
```yaml
bind-addresses:
- macaddress: ""
  interfacename: ""
  addresses:
  - hostname: ""
    address: 10.55.60.255
    cidr: ""
ingress-addresses:
- 10.55.60.255
```
For a particular relation, we get a little more information:
```
$ juju run --unit=dse/1 -- network-get cluster --format=yaml -r cluster:2
```
```yaml
bind-addresses:
- macaddress: ""
  interfacename: ""
  addresses:
  - hostname: ""
    address: 10.55.60.255
    cidr: ""
egress-subnets:
- 10.55.60.255/32
ingress-addresses:
- 10.55.60.255
```

We could easily expose this as the data structure returned by charmhelpers.core.hookenv.network_get().

Less easy would be readable, usable accessors to the useful bits. Designing this needs some more thought.

issue_title: GAM chunking uses too much memory
issue_body:
After a gam is produced for a fastq shard, it gets split up into chromosomes using vg chunk.  This step runs out of memory and crashes.  This is very likely because vg structures get created internally for all subgraphs, in this case, the whole genome.  

We can almost certainly fix this by repeatedly calling vg chunk.  Once for each path (or subset of paths). 

Will also look into optimizing vg chunk itself.

More details on crash here: https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/toil-vg/issues/69#issuecomment-277779696

issue_title: Add a way to check that mapping score goes up per read
issue_body:
I'm still trying to work out how to attach a per-read mapping score test to the Jenkins CI setup. It looks like the right place for the score extraction and comparison logic might be inside of toil-vg's `mapeval` mode. We're already using it to run alignments of the same reads against all the different kinds of graphs in the Jenkins tests, to make sure that the mapping positions are accurate. It wouldn't be too much harder to also feed `mapeval` the information that the mapping scores against one graph should always exceed the mapping scores against another graph. Alternately, we could tell it which graph to compare against, and it could spit out per-read score difference data for each other graph, and then the Jenkins test could scan through that and complain if any of the differences are negative.

@cmarkello, does this design make sense given what toil-vg's `mapeval` mode is supposed to do? Are you OK with me trying to turn it into a more general mapping evaluator, rather than just a tool to look at mapping positions?

issue_title: __class__ cell is defined for methods in py3
issue_body:
*Original report by [anntzer-lee](https://launchpad.net/~anntzer-lee) on [Launchpad](https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/1658411):*

------------------------------------

On Py3,

```python
class C:
    def ownclass(self):
        return __class__
print(C().ownclass())
```

executes correctly (`__class__` is the class in which the method was defined, which is not necessarily the type of `self`, see https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3135/#specification).  But pyflakes spuriously complains:

```
/tmp/foo.py:3: undefined name '__class__'
```

issue_title: OneTo{One,Many}Field.gen_value returns bad values
issue_body:
When `entity_obj.create_missing` is called, the `gen_value` method is called on all of that entity's required fields. Most fields, like names and descriptions, are easy to auto-generate. The `OneToOneField` and `OneToManyField` fields have a catch, though: the NailGun server configuration (`self.server_config_`) must be passed to the child entity. This ensures that both the parent and server object talk to the same server, use the same credentials, and so on.

The `OneToOneField.gen_value` and `OneToManyField.gen_value` methods fail tol pass a server configuration object to the entities that they create. They cannot reasonably do so, either, as a class attribute cannot (should not...) know about instance attributes. Instead of trying to fit a square peg into a round hole, just make `OneTo{One,Many}Field.gen_value` return a class instead of a full-blown object. This will solve this bug. And if the use cases in robottelo are any indication, client code will be better off.

This change is breaking, so it may be best to include this right before version 1.0.

issue_title: --dist=load runs at most half your tests at once
issue_body:
Because of this line, which distributes a minimum of two tests to each node: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-xdist/blob/master/xdist/dsession.py#L369

This came as a surprise to me, because this fact is undocumented, and py.test output doesn't make clear that this is happening. For users running slower tests, such as Selenium functional tests, full parallelization is often desirable. Could the minimum be changed to 1, or made configurable?

issue_title: Drop dependency on py?
issue_body: Should we strive to no longer explicitly depend on `py` in `pytest-xdist`? One thing in particular is `py.log`, which seems like it could/should be replaced by the standard `logging` module.

issue_title: pytest_unconfigure doesn't always complete on worker nodes
issue_body:
Hi,

It seems like pytest_unconfigure is called, but doesn't necessarily finish, on worker nodes.

Sample conftest.py:
```python
import time
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

def pytest_unconfigure(config):
    if hasattr(config, 'workerinput'):
        logging.info("Unconfiguring worker; sleeping")
        time.sleep(20)
        logging.info("Done")
    else:
        logging.info("Unconfiguring master; not sleeping")
        logging.info("Done")
```

Output:
```
$ py.test -s -n 1
========================================================================================================================================================================== test session starts ==========================================================================================================================================================================
platform darwin -- Python 2.7.14, pytest-3.3.2, py-1.5.2, pluggy-0.6.0
rootdir: /Users/mikemi/pytest_unconf_abort, inifile:
plugins: xdist-1.22.1, forked-0.2
gw0 [0]
scheduling tests via LoadScheduling
INFO:root:Unconfiguring worker; sleeping

===================================================================================================================================================================== no tests ran in 5.42 seconds ======================================================================================================================================================================
INFO:root:Unconfiguring master; not sleeping
INFO:root:Done
```

This is obviously just sample code, but I saw that a long running operation that I have in pytest_unconfigure sometimes didn't finish. Is there any way to make sure that the master blocks on all the workers' completions?

issue_title: Need to call `asyncio.set_event_loop()` in my tests
issue_body:
Hi there!

I'm porting some of my tests from v0.3.0 to v0.5.0 and I have a test that behaves strangely when I switch to v0.4.1 or later.

After some debugging, I noticed that `id(event_loop)` inside the test and `id(asyncio.get_event_loop())` return different results for versions after v0.4.1 and v0.5.0.

After adding a call to `asyncio.set_event_loop(event_loop)` in my tests like this:

```python
def test_serve(event_loop, cli):
    asyncio.set_event_loop(event_loop)  # wasn't required with v0.3.0.
```

Notice that I'm not using `@pytest.mark.asyncio` here.  I can't use it because I'm testing a [click](http://click.pocoo.org/) CLI that calls `event_loop.run_until_complete()` internally.

I guess this is explained by the fact that the `event_loop` fixture stopped calling `asyncio.set_event_loop()` when the `@pytest.mark.asyncio(forbid_global_event_loop=...)` syntax was introduced in v0.4.0.

In short, it seems like using only the `event_loop` fixture (e.g. without `@pytest.mark.asyncio`) leads to a surprising result.  I thing that the (absence of a) relationship between the `event_loop` fixture and `asyncio.get_event_loop()` should be added to the documentation to make it more obvious.  If you're OK with this, I can send in a PR to fix it.

In passing, I think there's a big quirk in the API here: the behaviour for using the `event_loop` fixture without the `asyncio` marker is surprising, to say to least.  The havior in v0.3.0 was much better than it is now in that respect.  This seems to be caused by the fact that `forbid_global_event_loop` is marker parameter rather than a fixture.  If the policy for controlling the relationship with the default event loop were a fixture, it would allow the `event_loop` fixture to decide what to do with the default event loop.

I've seen @asvetlov's comments in issue #12 and PR #24.  There seems to be some contention over whether returning exceptions from `asyncio.get_event_loop()` should be the default or not.  I don't have a specific position on this, but I am convinced that either it should return the same value as the `event_loop` fixture *or* it should fail (having two event loops by default is not ideal).  A neat side-effect of making the policy for the default event loop a fixture on which the `event_loop` fixture depends (instead of a parameter to the marker) would be to allow defining a project-specific default: each project would be able to define this new fixture as something returning a boolean (or enum value) to choose the policy and use `autouse` to make it a project specific default.

What are your thoughts on this?

issue_title: Support for headless Chrome
issue_body:
Is there a possibility to run tests on Chrome in headless mode that I'm missing from the documentation? In standard selenium I can ensure that by: 
```python
options = webdriver.ChromeOptions()
options.add_argument('headless')
driver = webdriver.Chrome(chrome_options=options)
```

I guessed `capabilities` could be the thing I need, so I tried: `pytest --capability headless True`  but that didn't work. By hacking on the source code of pytest-selenium I was able to use Chrome in headless mode - I modified the `chrome_options` fixture:
```python
@pytest.fixture
def chrome_options():
    options = Options()
    options.add_argument('headless')
    return options
```
This might be working for me on my local machine, but it creates a lot of hassle to ensure that in my staging environment....

issue_body:
I'm attempting to use the F5 SDK to get the sync-status for a BIG-IP system. This is defined by the sync-status resource of the CM module. Here's the complete code from LBaaS v1 that I'm attempting to re-write using the SDK:

    def get_sync_status(self):
        """ Get the sync status description for the bigip """
        request_url = self.bigip.icr_url + '/cm/sync-status?$select=status'
        response = self.bigip.icr_session.get(request_url,
                                              timeout=const.CONNECTION_TIMEOUT)
        if response.status_code < 400:
            response_obj = json.loads(response.text)
            entries = response_obj['entries']
            status = entries['https://localhost/mgmt/tm/cm/sync-status/0']
            desc = status['nestedStats']['entries']['status']['description']
            return desc
        else:
            Log.error('sync-status', response.text)
            raise exceptions.ClusterQueryException(response.text)
        return None

issue_title: Category handling overhaul bucket
issue_body:
At my current job, we're running into some issues with category handling. I get some time to improve the state of things in Oscar.

Goals:
- Easily allow editing slugs independently of names. slugs are important for SEO and might need to match legacy URLs, so the auto-generating of slugs should be an optional step. (#561)
- Address MySQL size limits (#1119). 

I haven't thought it through just yet, but I think removing the ``full_slug``/full name denormalization is a good first step, as it nicely simplifies the slug updating process. I hope that we can address any performance issues with a simple cache.

I'll attempt to watch out for:
- not increasing query counts considerably
- not breaking existing URLs

Bonus goals:
- Ensure that it's easy to translate categories.
- Ensure that it's easy to display them alphabetically.
- Ensure that it's easy to not display categories without products.

Further reading:
- https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/pull/322 worked towards editing the slugs independently. But we're seeing issues where the parent's save method still overrides the slugs.

Switch le-auto symlink to copy instead.

```
Select the appropriate number [1-2] then [enter] (press 'c' to cancel): 2
```

In order for me to run this in a cron job I would like this to be documented how I can pre-seed the answers. I found --replace, but documentation could be a lot clearer on how to make this all automatic.

issue_title: Don't make privatekey world readable
issue_body: The private key is currently created with o+r (others+read) permissions. The protection is done by removing the executable flag from the directory. Please set an explicit umask when (auto)creating the privatekey.

issue_title: "SSLEngine on" does not appear to be an exhausitve/correct test for SSL vhosts
issue_body:
Over in #1423 @ArchimedesPi noticed that the [value set for `is_ssl`](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt-apache/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L433), based on whether `SSLEngine on` is in the vhost entry, is wrong.  In fact, it seems to be wrong for the SSL vhosts that `letsencrypt-apache` creates itself!

Probably we should be also checking for whether the vhost ends in :443!

issue_title: TypeError: coercing to Unicode: need string or buffer, NoneType found
issue_body:
Since https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/issues/1081 is still a thing, I copied my configuration (from ~2 months ago) from the server to local machine and am trying to renew the certificate.

Running 

```
sudo letsencrypt --work-dir ~/Desktop/letsencrypt/ certonly --standalone --config-dir ~/Desktop/letsencrypt/
```

will allow one to enter domains at which point it outputs 

```
An unexpected error occurred:
TypeError: coercing to Unicode: need string or buffer, NoneType found
Please see the logfiles in /var/log/letsencrypt for more details.
```

and quits. Logs are empty.

issue_title: Sphinx docs no longer build off of PyPi
issue_body:
Well, this is a bit tricky.  Because the sphinx documentation build process reads the actual code to help generate the docs, we are seeing problems when using the tarballs that come out of PyPi.  The documentation makes references to code that's no longer shipped - letsencrypt_apache, letsencrypt_nginx, letsencrypt_compatibility_test, and letshelp_letsencrypt_*.

I'm not entirely sure how to solve this.  If we move the documentation that refers to the other modules into those tarballs and try to build it there, will it build correctly based on the code installed in the system paths?

@kuba, @fmarier, I'd love both of your input here.

issue_title: Support "http-01" ACME challenge
issue_body: The existing "simpleHttp" and "dvsni" challenges are insecure, and boulder will be disabling them shortly.  The client needs to implement the new "http-01" challenge in order to be compatible with future boulder.

issue_title: poll_and_request_issuance loops forever on failure
issue_body:
In https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/acme/acme/client.py#L326, we only check for STATUS_VALID. We should also check for STATUS_INVALID and return once that happens, or there will be many clients looping forever making requests to the server.

Similarly, that method should have a timeout.

issue_title: Persistence of .pyc files causes periodic local tox failures
issue_body: I just wasted a bunch of time chasing down another instance of this. We should probably rm them in the `dev/venv.sh` script, at the very least.

issue_title: When reissuing a revoked cert, the client prompts you to keep the old one
issue_body:
I generated a couple Let's Encrypt certs with the default settings. Then I realized that the default RSA key size is 2048, whereas I prefer to be paranoid and use 4096-bit keys. No problem! I just revoked the old certs and went to reissue new ones, passing `--rsa-key-size`.

It was at this point that I got a prompt telling me that I had a certificate with exactly the same domain name on it, and did I want to keep the existing cert? This is obviously wrong as the certificate is revoked and thus no longer useful.

```
% letsencrypt --version                                                                                                  
letsencrypt 0.4.1

% apt-cache policy letsencrypt
letsencrypt:
  Installed: 0.4.1-1~bpo8+1
  Candidate: 0.4.1-1~bpo8+1
  Version table:
 *** 0.4.1-1~bpo8+1 0
        100 http://ftp.us.debian.org/debian/ jessie-backports/main amd64 Packages
        100 /var/lib/dpkg/status
```

issue_title: Add peep and a requirements file with hashes.
issue_body: As per https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/issues/1185#issuecomment-158210841, embed peep, and add a requirements file containing the union of requirements of all the packages letsencrypt-auto installs. letsencrypt-auto will then use peep instead of pip.

issue_title: Implement nonces for replay protection
issue_body:
Here's the pull request implementing nonces in Boulder:
https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/pull/325

Here's the relevant spec section:
https://github.com/letsencrypt/acme-spec/blob/master/draft-barnes-acme.md#replay-protection

issue_title: Auth command with --csr flag fails
issue_body:
Example command would be 
```bash
sudo -H ./letsencrypt-auto auth --csr ../dev.usterix.com.csr
```
where the path to the CSR is a csr that you have generated.

issue_title: Expose error_cb to Python
issue_body:
Proposed Enhancement:

For both the Consumer and Producer, it seems necessary to expose the error_cb in the Python API so that Consumer and Producer code can deal with errors, especially w.r.t. connections with Kafka.

For example, consider the Consumer pseudo code:

```
while True:
    msg = consumer.poll(1.0)
    if not msg:
        logger.info('No messages found in polling window')
        continue
    if msg.error():
        logger.exception('Received error', error=msg.error())
        raise confluent_kafka.KafkaException(msg.error())
```

This code will run forever, even if the consumer loses connection to the brokers, which isn't always what you want. In my case, I'd like to fail if the error has persisted for the past 5 minutes.

It seems like it wouldn't be too much work to do something similar to what is done for the `on_revoke` and `on_assign` callbacks, but not sure about the intricacies of the librdkafka's error_cb.

This is blocking us from adopting confluent-kafka-python more broadly (or at all).

Thanks for your time.

issue_title: Message value has payload argument that causes ide warning
issue_body:
Description
===========
The returned Kafka messages value method expects an argument `payload`
that makes the ide mark the calling of the method as problematic. It's not a real issue but 
I wonder where it stems from.

for instance

```python
msg = consumer.poll()
process_msg_value(msg.value())
```
will give a warning in pycharm as if I am missing an input on the value method.

The method I find is the following

```python

    def value(self, payload): # real signature unknown; restored from __doc__
        """
        :returns: message value (payload) or None if not available.
          :rtype: str|bytes or None
        """
        pass

```

How to reproduce
================
On pycharm (my version 2017.1) call `msg.value()`


Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [ ] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`): 0.9.2
 - [ ] Apache Kafka broker version:
 - [ ] Client configuration: `{...}`
 - [ ] Operating system: MacOS
 - [ ] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)
 - [ ] Provide broker log excerpts
 - [ ] Critical issue

issue_title: Speedup: Don't put args in List(); use vector()
issue_body:
Apply's looks like this:

1. put args in list
2. call apply, apply-cont, etc.
3. call `list->vector(args)`
4. `f(*args)`

I think we can just put them in a vector to begin with.

issue_title: parse() converts a str tzoffset name to unicode
issue_body:
In dateutil 2.4.2 (and HEAD) `dateutil.parser.parse()` converts the supplied tzoffset name to a unicode() object 

```python
>>> from dateutil.parser import parse
>>> parse("2012-01-19 17:21:00 BRST", tzinfos={"BRST": -10800})
datetime.datetime(2012, 1, 19, 17, 21, tzinfo=tzoffset(u'BRST', -10800))
```

This differs from the behaviour of `tzoffset`

```python
>>> from dateutil.tz import tzoffset
>>> tzoffset("BRST", -10800)
tzoffset('BRST', -10800)
```

I came across this whilst trying to incorporate the contents of docs/ (and hence docstrings) into the CI builds. I just want to check the behaviour is a bug, i.e. not by design.

issue_title: Accept more than 6 digits for fractional seconds in isoparse
issue_body:
Currently, `isoparse` raises an exception if there are more than 6 digits in the fractional second.

```
>>> dateutil.parser.isoparse("2006-01-02T15:04:05.9999999-07:00")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/jay/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dateutil/parser/isoparser.py", line 37, in func
    return f(self, str_in, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/jay/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dateutil/parser/isoparser.py", line 136, in isoparse
    components += self._parse_isotime(dt_str[pos + 1:])
  File "/home/jay/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dateutil/parser/isoparser.py", line 363, in _parse_isotime
    raise ValueError('Unused components in ISO string')
ValueError: Unused components in ISO string
```

Go, for example, includes up to 9 digits (nanosecond precision) when marshaling a time into JSON. It would be nice if `isoparse` could accept those without issue, even if precision gets truncated a bit.

issue_title: Resolving absolute http URIs from file base path breaks on Windows
issue_body:
The fixes in #120 added a regression, as now on win32 the below resolution of an absolute path is wrong:

```python
fetcher.urljoin("file:///C:/Users/fred/foo.cwl", "http://example.com/bar/soup.cwl")
```

returns `file://example.com/bar/soup.cwl` instead of the `http` URI as-is.

The [code in question](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/schema_salad/pull/120/files) is kind-of trying to follow [RFC 8089 Appendix E.2](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc8089#appendix-E.2) - which highlight another edge case:

```python
fetcher.urljoin("file:///d:/baz/soup.cwl", "/foo/soup.cwl")
```

should then preserve the `d:` drive and become `file:///d:/foo/soup.cwl`

issue_title: Client: Don't use cache by default
issue_body:
Client tries to be overly helpful by caching by default. This is handy functionality, but users should enable this themselves.

Either disable by default, or consider renaming `cache_path = dir` to `cache = False|True|str`, where False is no caching, True is default path (current behavior), and str is custom path.

issue_title: Add is_published field to Event model
issue_body:
In order to distinguish between published and unpublished events, an `is_published` boolean field should be added to the event model. It's default value should be false, as all new events are treated as drafts.

After making this change, the API should be updated so that unpublished events (`is_published=False`) are only displayed to authenticated users. This can be done by adding the `DispatchPublishableMixin` to the `EventsViewSet` and adding [this line](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/apps/api/views.py#L58) to the `get_queryset()` method. The `get_publishable_queryset()` method automatically checks for the right permissions and filters the queryset if needed.

issue_title: spotify segment in vim radically slows down vim
'auto' watcher exception loop
issue_body:
When `TreeWatcher.get_watcher` is called and watcher_type is set to `auto`, exceptions that are handled with try/except keep getting hit over and over again.

Specifically, I'm running into an inotify `DirTooLarge` in my main git repo, and the time/resources spent trying to return an `INotifyTreeWatcher` each time are wasteful.

I think it would be beneficial to reset `self.watcher_type` based on the first successful initialized watcher when we init `TreeWatcher` with `watcher_type=auto`

issue_title: Instance fields
issue_body:
Is it possible to have fields specified(defined) by a schema instance(e.g. in a schema's `__init__` method)? 

If not, is there a reason why that shouldn't/couldn't be possible? 

The use case for this is the same as that of any class: to have the class specify attributes that are unchanging accross instances, but to have instances differ among each other on some attributes.

For example, to define a class of schemas for which some fields depend on another schema to be chosen at runtime:

```
class DependentSchema(Schema):
    def __init__(self, dependency, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.dependent_field = dependency.fields["a_field"]

    static_field = fields.Field() 
    class Meta:
        fields = ("static_field", "dependent_field")
```


Thanks.

issue_title: Empty Only Treated as None
issue_body:
When the `only` parameter is an empty list/set, it causes all the fields to be de/serialized like `None`. The implementation of 2.x and 3.0 are not in compliance with their respective docs.

> **only** (_tuple_) – A list or tuple of fields to serialize. If **None**, all fields will be serialized.

```py
class TestSchema(Schema):
    foo = fields.Field()
sch = TestSchema(only=())
data = dict(foo='bar')
result = sch.dump(data)
assert 'foo' not in result

# assert 'foo' not in {'foo': 'bar'}
```

This could create a security vulnerability if an application was dynamically generating the field set based on security role. A filter that was meant to hide everything would inadvertently show everything.

issue_title: fields.Constant is confusing
issue_body:
`fields.Contsant` only works for fields which are already present in the object.
```python
from marshmallow import fields, Schema, pprint

class Test(Schema):
    field = fields.Constant(0)
    field2 = fields.Constant(1)

pprint(Test().load({'field': 1}))
```

I'd expect this code to give me:
```
UnmarshalResult(data={'field': 0, 'field2': 1}, errors={})
```
But this is what I get:
```
UnmarshalResult(data={'field': 0}, errors={})
```

If this is the expected behavior, it isn't really clear from the docs.
So either the docs or the behavior ought to be changed.

issue_title: Allow load to add missing fields with default values
issue_body:
When I have something like this:

```
from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class UserSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.Str(required=True)
    email = fields.Email(required=True)
    created_at = fields.DateTime()
    some_other = fields.Str(default='This is something else')
    
user_data = {
    'created_at': '2014-08-11T05:26:03.869245',
    'email': u'ken@yahoo.com',
    'name': u'Ken'
}
schema = UserSchema()
result = schema.load(user_data)
pprint(result)
```

I currently get:

```
{'created_at': datetime.datetime(2014, 8, 11, 5, 26, 3, 869245),
 'email': 'ken@yahoo.com',
 'name': 'Ken'}
```

I would expect that `some_other` would be added with the default set to `This is something else`, especially if we are passing this to `@post_load` to create an object so that I don't have to have said object also handle setting the default (and potentially getting out of sync).

This is especially handy when using marshmallow to validate and transform the data as necessary on a load before passing it further down the stack to avoid having to add a bunch of `if key in obj` statements.

Currently I end up doing:

```
result = schema.dump(schema.load(user_data))
pprint(result)
```

Which does have the desired result:

```
{'created_at': '2014-08-11T05:26:03.869245+00:00',
 'email': 'ken@yahoo.com',
 'name': 'Ken',
 'some_other': 'This is something else'}
```

but is doing a lot of extra work that shouldn't be necessary.

----

Is there already a recipe for doing something like this with a `@post_load` decorator? If so I don't mind using that.

issue_title: Symbolic names containing operand suffixes can be missleading
issue_body:
When data comes from memory or calldata the operands that are appended to describe the index should be simplified, and if still symbolic should be surrounded with brackets. If not some constraints or symbolic contents are ambiguous:
BitVec("mem_" + str(op0), 256) **->** mem_128 + calldatavalue_contractname_someindex **better** mem[128 + calldatavalue_contractname_someindex] **or** mem_at[128 + calldatavalue_contractname_someindex]

state.stack.append(BitVec("calldata_" + str(environment.active_account.contract_name) + "_" + str(op0), 256)) -> calldata_4 + 32 **better** calldata[4+32] **or** calldata_at[4+32] **with simplify** calldata[36] and calldata_at[36] respectively

issue_title: Plan for non-Beta release
issue_body:
TastyPie has been largely stable for several years now, and probably deserves a 1.0 release.  This issue is to start the conversation about what should be included in that milestone.

1. Bugs
-- there aren't many of these that are still pressing concerns, but there are ancient issues going back to the beginning.  It'd be a herculean effort to verify all of these, but ones that appear to be **major** could be labelled as such and verified fixed and tested.
-- **Minor** bugs from before 0.14's release can probably be closed, unless they're easy to confirm and fix. 
2. Features
-- I have some requests of my own, about making some bits easier to work with.  Off the top of my head, reversing a URL (e.g. during testing) is more painful than it needs to be.  Automatic logging would also help debugging efforts a great deal.
-- The community at large probably has their own feature requests.  There might be some buried in the ~300 existing issues, but another huge effort could dig through closed issues (many of which are support requests) looking for common themes suggesting fit & finish improvements.
3. Documentation
-- The existing docs are pretty good.  But I'm fairly certain that some of the common themes in existing issues could be solved with documentation improvements.  This is where new users are particularly helpful - problems they run into are usage obstacles the veteran users have learned to deal with semi-automatically.

Anything else?

issue_title: Validate model topology for inplace layer/operations
issue_body:
Some layer has inplace option, which means the input will be modified during the forward process. But sometimes, the inplace layer is dangerous to use:

1. Put inplace layer as the first layer in the model. So the training data is changed, while the training data should be immutable during the whole training process.
2. A layer point to several layers and one of them is inplace layer. The may cause an incorrect result.

So we should verify the model topology for these inplace layers

           f.write(s_time+"\t"+str(f_zenith)+"\n")
           
           
    
    
f.close()
#-------------

As the source data (coordinates, time zone and GHI), I use the model data of the SAM application (System Advisor Model).


But when I try to calc DNI and DHI via pvlib, my findings are completely different from those of the model.

For example, in the model on the first day of the year at 10 o'clock, the values of GHI, DNI, DHI are as follows:
GHI: 457
DNI: 901
DHI: 53

The calculated zenith angle is equal to 66.9732005775

I try to calculate the DNI and GHI as follows:
#---------------------

#day of year
doy=1
i_ghi=457
f_zenith=66.9732005775


f_dni=pvlib.irradiance.erbs(i_ghi,f_zenith,doy)["dni"]         
f_dhi=pvlib.irradiance.erbs(i_ghi,f_zenith,doy)["dhi"]


#---------------------

The values of DNI and GHI are as follows:
DNI: 975.5429981466746
GHI: 75.405


These values are far from the values of model.
How much do I correctly calculate?

issue_title: JSON_API_FORMAT_KEYS setting to default to lowercase plural type?
issue_body:
While the [spec](http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-member-names) allows upper and lower case characters in `type` names, the [recommendations](http://jsonapi.org/recommendations/#naming) suggest all lowercase. The choice of plural vs. singular for the `type` is undefined but should be used consistently and most examples use plural.

Since PEP8 wants class names to be CapWords, I can enforce a "local standard" such as lowercase plurals with `JSONAPIMeta` `resource_name` in the models.Model like this:
```python
class Widget(Common):
    name = models.TextField()
    qty = models.PositiveIntegerField(blank=True, default=0)

    class Meta:
        ordering = ["name"]

    class JSONAPIMeta:
        resource_name = "widgets"

    def __str__(self):
        return self.name + ' (' + str(self.id) + ')'
```
However this is not DRY.

Alternatively I think I can set these:
```python
JSON_API_FORMAT_KEYS = 'camelize'
JSON_API_PLURALIZE_TYPES = True
```
But `camelize` is not the same as all lowercase.

Would it make sense to add a `JSON_API_FORMAT_KEYS` option for `lowercase` to add to the current list of `('dasherize', 'camelize', 'underscore', 'capitalize')`?

issue_title: Right way to pass dataset to the main function?
issue_body:
What is the right way to pass train dataset to the sacred 'main' function?
Dataset could be too big to keep it in the configuration.

Taking into account that I'm trying to use sacred on top of the previously implemented Keras-powered modules, the project structure looks like this (kind of pseudocode):
```
ex = Experiment()

def train(args, params):
    dataset, params = prepare(args, params)
    config(params) 
    ex.run()

def config(params):
    ex.add_config(file)
    ex.add_config(params)

@ex.main
def sacred_main():
    # Do something
    sacred_captured_fn(dataset)
    # Do something

@ex.capture
def sacred_captured_fn(dataset):
    model = really_build_model(params)
    result = really_train_model(model, params)

def main(cmd_args):    ## Real main function
   # Do something
   train(args, params)
```

Should I use global variables?

issue_title: Best practice for running multiple (possible parallel) experiments
Using the experiment name in captured functions and/or config
issue_body: As noted in #110, I'd like to be able to derive config entries (mostly file names and thelike) from the experiment name. So far I can accomplish that with a `tag` config entry, but that seems very redundant and doesn't show up nicely in sacredboard.

issue_title: Return error code when something goes wrong
issue_body:
For example, I use `berry` like this in a script:

``` sh
berry -f berry.yaml --once creds/
```

And when there is a mistake (wrong `application_id`) in berry.yaml, it complains, but still returns 0, so that the script doesn't stop.

It would be useful to return 1 or other error-codes when an error occurs.

issue_title: WeightedDnsElasticLoadBalancer doesn't remove MainDomain
issue_body:
It seems like if you make changes to the `MainDomain` resources of a `WeightedDnsElasticLoadBalancer` it will not get deleted on `senza delete`.

1. Create a stack with a `WeightedDnsElasticLoadBalancer`
2. Shift some traffic to your stack with `senza traffic` (or by changing the weight of the DNS record in the AWS console)
3. Delete the stack with `senza del`

CF will show an event that the `MainDomain` record was removed successfully, but Route53 will still show the record after deletion of the stack has finished.

Since deletion of the record works if you _reset_ the weight of the record back to zero (as it is specified in the CF template for this record) it looks like CF removes only those resources which fit all parameters as specified in the template. It's still strange the CF reports the removal as a success.

Do we have to update resources of a stack (e.g. changing DNS weights) via CF `update_stack`?

issue_title: Make a pre-release
issue_body:
Since [this feature request](https://github.com/symengine/symengine/issues/1341) was implemented, I migrated my modules
[JiTCODE](https://github.com/neurophysik/jitcode),
[JiTCDDE](https://github.com/neurophysik/jitcdde), and
[JiTCSDE](https://github.com/neurophysik/jitcdde)
to from using SymPy to SymEngine for symbolics and C code printing. The outcome is truly awesome: Previously, the duration of the code-generation step was a major drawback of these modules. Now, it is sped up by a factor of up to nine hundred, making it negligible in most situations.

Except for overhauling the documentation and some unrelated changes, I am finished with the new versions. Therefore, I am confident that I have no further needs for changes to SymEngine. Now, when I release the new version, it would be great if my users didn’t have to bother with building and installing SymEngine and SymEngine.py by hand, but could pull it directly from PyPI as a requirement. Would it be possible to make a pre-release, new release, or similar including [this commit](https://github.com/symengine/symengine/commit/72de6b22e1ec1e5b10b39a400cdafd54795f5bc0) to PyPI?

issue_title: Assigning a value to function based on that function value gives unexpected results.
issue_body:
A bit of a mouthful title, and truth be told I'm unsure if this is a bug in the program or a bug in my thinking.

```
(declare-fun Temp () Int)
(assert(= Temp (ite (= Temp 0) 1 Temp)))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
(exit)
```

Initially:
> (-INF < Temp < INF)

So, I would expect the evaluation of 
> (= Temp 0)

To come first, and as it is true, the assertion used is:
> (assert(= Temp 1 ))

Instead, it would seem Temp is being assigned any value (albeit a constant one on each run of the program), which would lead me to believe that the assertion that is being applied is:
> (assert(= Temp Temp))

What is the cause of this?
