issue_title: [TarFS] Consider separating out getinfo_from_member(member)
issue_body:
Hi,

In my use case I have 2 requirements:
- walk a tree where *.tar.gz, *.zip and possibly others are treated like directories
- open possibly large tar-files in streaming mode="r|*" and avoid jumping backwards and forwards within that file. This requirement is also the reason why my walker must return a file descriptor (fd) and ResourceInfo

I've implemented a walker that makes heavy use of yours and transparently handles the container files (*.tar.*) when such a file is found. It yields the fd and ResourceInfo exactly as wanted. Except that I had to copy a piece of your code from tarfs.py to create the Info object for tar file entries. Remember I'm opening the tar file myself and I'm (sequentially) iterating through it myself. Yet I still want to consistently return an Info object.

I called my new function getinfo_from_member(member) which is more or less a copy of the 2nd half of getinfo(). Remember that I'm not able to use the tarfs.getinfo() implementation because it "seeks" the information in the tar file, which I'm not able (don't want) to do in streaming mode.

It would be great if I didn't have to copy the source code but call a tarfs method instead. Do think you could separate it out?

thanks a lot
Juergen

issue_title: CodeCov not running some tests
issue_body:
## 1) Bug

CodeCov might not be accurately calculating coverage. For example, it is showing that the [HDF5IO class never gets constructed:](https://codecov.io/gh/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/src/dev/src/pynwb/form/backends/hdf5/h5tools.py#L24)

But, this test is calling the constructor. 
https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/blob/4c450bb9d6470cd79ddcec16555d640008e3f1f0/tests/unit/form_tests/test_io_hdf5.py#L201

I did some more digging through the individual files, and it seems like things are getting loaded (declarations get hit), but tests are never getting executed.

See [pynwb.form.build.builders](https://codecov.io/gh/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/src/dev/src/pynwb/form/build/builders.py)

## Checklist

- [ ] Have you ensured the feature or change was not already [reported](https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/issues/) ?
- [ ] Have you included a brief and descriptive title?
- [ ] Have you included a clear description of the problem you are trying to solve?
- [ ] Have you included a minimal code snippet that reproduces the issue you are encountering?

issue_title: Empty "accessed_data_references" in CFGNode?
issue_body: Hi! I am trying to analyze the "encrypt" function of a binary to figure out all possible memory offsets of char array "key", assuming that I already know the exact location of the base address of "encrypt". ``` void encrypt(FILE *in, FILE *out, char *key){ /* initialize two buffers (in & out), passes key into OpenSSL struct, and encrypts file block by block */ } ``` Here's my script: ``` cfg = project.analyses.CFGAccurate(keep_state=True) cfg_fast = project.analyses.CFGFast() func_manager = cfg.functions funcs = func_manager.values() # Currently I'm running on a binary w/ debugger info included, so I can do: encrypt_func = [func for func in funcs if func.name == 'encrypt'][0] /* skipping steps to get relevant blocks and cfg_nodes of interest */ blocks = ... cfg_nodes = ... cfg_fast_nodes = ... ``` However: - It seems that the `cfg_fast_node.accessed_data_references` are all empty; - `project.analyses.VariableRecovery` doesn't contain data on heap, per #1173 ; and - `cfg_node.[input_state + final_states].solver.get_variables('mem')` seem to only yield uninitialized memory. Am I doing something wrong, and is there any way to achieve what I want to do with angr? Thanks!

issue_title: BlockNode can be missing in Function block list
issue_body:
Hello, Apparently, in some code (code that has exceptions, or code that initializes hardware for example), we can encounter this kind of function call: ``` 00008008 : 8008: 4b08 ldr r3, [pc, #32] ; (802c ) 800a: 4809 ldr r0, [pc, #36] ; (8030 ) 800c: 4283 cmp r3, r0 800e: d20a bcs.n 8026 8010: 43da mvns r2, r3 8012: 2100 movs r1, #0 8014: 4402 add r2, r0 8016: f022 0203 bic.w r2, r2, #3 801a: 3204 adds r2, #4 801c: 441a add r2, r3 801e: f843 1b04 str.w r1, [r3], #4 8022: 4293 cmp r3, r2 8024: d1fb bne.n 801e 8026: f000 b83b b.w 80a0
``` Function `Main` is called in `ResetISR` using a `b` instruction instead of `bl`. Of course, VEX does not detect the call and `CFGAccurate` does not either. The problem might be just that there will not be any `Function` object for `Main`, and all basic blocks of `Main` will be in `Function` of `ResetISR`. But it can also be that a `Function` object does not contain any blocks. Here's an example: ``` 0000a220 : a220: 6041 str r1, [r0, #4] a222: 4770 bx lr ``` This function only has one single block. It is called from different places: - Here with a `b`, the function call is not detected ``` 0000969c : 969c: b538 push {r3, r4, r5, lr} 969e: 4605 mov r5, r0 96a0: 4c07 ldr r4, [pc, #28] ; (96c0 ) 96a2: 2200 movs r2, #0 96a4: 4620 mov r0, r4 96a6: 213d movs r1, #61 ; 0x3d 96a8: f000 fdae bl a208 96ac: 4629 mov r1, r5 96ae: 4620 mov r0, r4 96b0: f000 fdc4 bl a23c 96b4: 4620 mov r0, r4 96b6: e8bd 4038 ldmia.w sp!, {r3, r4, r5, lr} 96ba: 2103 movs r1, #3 96bc: f000 bdb0 b.w a220 ``` - Or here with a `bl`, but since the `BlockNode` is already in the `OSRAMWriteFirst` `Function` object, the `Function` `I2CMasterControl` is created but has no block. ``` 96ec: 2101 movs r1, #1 96ee: 4620 mov r0, r4 96f0: f000 fd96 bl a220 ``` So, I don't know what would be the best way to fix this. Adding more logic to reason about jumpkind? Or we can just add more test in `Function` class, like here. I guess this is a @ltfish issue. Please let me know what you think

issue_title: Question about creating python modules of the macro functions
issue_body:
Hi folks.
In the examples from the tutorial, some of the macro functions, like goto(), arm_and_takeoff(), are rather useful but I found it a bit messy to put their definitions in the main script. So I was thinking about creating a separate module (say module A) to define them and import the module in the main script. 
However, as I was trying with it, since some of these functions have the dependency of the "vehicle" instance that is defined as "vehicle = connect(connection_string, wait_ready=True)" in the script, this could not be done easily. The interpreter of Python runs the imported module first before importing (rather than just copying the definitions of the functions into the main script and compiling like a C compiler), and there does not exist a similar functionality of "extern variables" as in C. 
Then I tried to connect to the vehicle (SITL) and define the "vehicle" instance directly in module A or import "vehicle" from the main script into module A, but neither works. Even though the scripts could be interpreted and run successfully, there was no heartbeat. I am supposing this is caused by the fact that the vehicle is connected twice respectively by the main script thread and the module thread.
So I was wondering whether there is a way to solve this problem? It would be really convenient to maintain multiple scripts if the functions are defined only in a single module.

issue_title: urls will break on 'narrow' python 2.7-3.3 builds
issue_body:
Found this package via the twisted mailing list, was surprised to see a nexus of glyph, mahmoud  and cory so had to peek...

I just found/fixed an issue in a another python package, tldextract, and this one is afflicted by the same issue as well.

I'll link to my bug report and fix for the other project below (which i documented a lot and reference PEPs to), but here's the TLDR:

* Python2.7 through 3.3 store unicode data as UCS-2 or UCS-4, it's a compile-time option and UCS-2 is the default.  Python 3.3+ stores unicode data differently.  a lot of linux/mac distributions shipped with a UCS-2 compile option.

* UC-2 is considered a "narrow" build and has a max character range of 65535

    >>> import sys
    >>> print sys.maxunicode

* many punycode encodings will break during unicode decoding...

    >>> import hyperlink
    >>> url =  'https://xn--vi8hiv.ws'
    >>> obj = hyperlink.URL.from_text(url)
    >>> print obj.to_iri()
    ValueError: unichr() arg not in range(0x10000) (narrow Python build)

Catch that error?  it's a ValueError, not a UnicodeEncodeError.  

Fixing that is pretty easy... https://github.com/mahmoud/hyperlink/blob/master/hyperlink/_url.py#L1012-L1015

         try:
             asciiHost = self.host.encode("ascii")
         except UnicodeEncodeError:
             textHost = self.host
    +    except ValueError:
    +        textHost = self.host

but it may make sense to add some more tests with a broken domain (like the f*&^#*&^s link emoji url above, which has broken my indexer an ungodly number of times)

everything is explained in detail here:
   https://github.com/john-kurkowski/tldextract/issues/122

and here:
    https://github.com/john-kurkowski/tldextract/pull/130/files

issue_title: [Question] A2C entropy of the policy
issue_body:
Hi all,

I am having a very hard time trying to understand how do we compute the gradient of the entropy with respect to the actor parameters in continuous action domains. This gradient is added to the policy gradient in A3C paper to discourage too quick convergence toward suboptimal deterministic policies.

It would require a integration over the action space so it seems to me that it is intractable in many cases. Is the integral sampled?

I looked at the code in baselines/common/distributions and i am completely lost, I do not understand many of the notations (Pd = policy distribution? , flat = ? , neglogp = negative log probability? ) and why is the name of the class Diag Gaussian? Are we assuming the policy distribution to be Gaussian in every continuous action domains?

Is there a documentation about this code that i missed?

Thanks a lot for any information
Cheers!

issue_title: Can't use custom field as input in mutation
issue_body:
I have a custom field (written with @syrusakbary help in [this issue](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/issues/433)):

```python
from functools import partial

class ProtoEnum(graphene.Field):
    def __init__(self, protobuf_enum, *args, **kwargs):
        super(ProtoEnum, self).__init__(graphene.Int, *args, **kwargs)
        self.protobuf_enum = protobuf_enum

    def get_resolver(self, parent_resolver):
        return partial(_enum_resolver, self.protobuf_enum, parent_resolver)

def _enum_resolver(protobuf_enum, parent_resolver, root, args, context, info):
    resolved_value = parent_resolver(root, args, context, info)
    if resolved_value not in protobuf_enum.values():
        raise ValueError('Enum value "%s" is invalid' % resolved_value)
    return resolved_value
```

I wanted to use it as a input in my mutation like so:

```python
class Input:
    my_field = ProtoEnum(...)
```
Which gave me this error:

```
File "third_party/python3/graphene/types/field.py", line 54, in __init__
  File "third_party/python3/graphene/types/argument.py", line 61, in to_arguments
ValueError: Expected my_field to be Argument, but received ProtoEnum. Try using Argument(Int!).
```

I tried wrapping it in `Argument`, which in turn gave me this error:

```
File "third_party/python3/graphene/types/schema.py", line 44, in __init__
  File "third_party/python3/graphene/types/schema.py", line 105, in build_typemap
  File "third_party/python3/graphene/types/typemap.py", line 65, in __init__
  File "third_party/python3/graphql/type/typemap.py", line 16, in __init__
  File "third_party/python3/graphene/types/typemap.py", line 73, in reducer
  File "third_party/python3/graphene/types/typemap.py", line 103, in graphene_reducer
  File "third_party/python3/graphql/type/typemap.py", line 79, in reducer
  File "third_party/python3/graphql/pyutils/cached_property.py", line 16, in __get__
  File "third_party/python3/graphql/type/definition.py", line 180, in fields
  File "third_party/python3/graphql/type/definition.py", line 189, in define_field_map
  File "third_party/python3/graphene/types/typemap.py", line 246, in construct_fields_for_type
  File "third_party/python3/graphene/types/typemap.py", line 295, in get_field_type
AttributeError: 'ProtoEnum' object has no attribute '_meta'
```

I tried adding a `Meta` class but that didn't do anything. I can use custom field just fine in my schema otherwise.

Am I doing something wrong or is this just not supported?

issue_title: Create API backcompatibility tests
issue_body: TensorFlow has a rather thorough [API backwards compatibility test](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/tree/master/tensorflow/tools/api/tests) which ensures that backwards-incompatible changes are not accidentally made to the API. We should implement similar (ideally copied and simplified) test infrastructure before our official 1.0 debut.

issue_title: Global tagging of CloudFormation stacks
issue_body: It would be nice to have a section in the stack configuration where one can set tags applied to all stacks defined in the config.

issue_title: Implement MPL delayed retransmissions.
issue_body: The current MPL implementation retransmits MPL messages only once and without delay.  The implementation needs to be enhanced such that a router retransmits each MPL message multiple times with a randomized delay.

Can not doing so cause issues?

issue_title: Missing jitters for responses to MLE Parent Request/Link Request
issue_body:
Both Thread Specifications and Certification Test Plan define that responses for MLE Parent Requests must be delayed by a random jitter (up to 500 ms for Router only request, and up to 1000 ms for the second request). I'm not sure if this is currenty covered by test plan, it may be in the final version. The same applies for responses to multicast MLE Link Requests (up to 1000 ms).

Also I have a question regarding Mle::BecomeChild function. Why is the initial MLE Parent Request delayed by 1000 ms? Is this a overlook, or are there any implementation-specific reasons for that?

issue_title: Case 7.1.3 is failed because the expected packet "unicast data response" is not found (refer to Issue #2107)
issue_body:
Hi,
When I test case 7.1.3  using openthread according to test plan V1.1, the test procedure 2  "
The DUT MUST send a unicast MLE Data Response with the updatedNetwork Data to SED_1." is failed.
I find that in function "SynchronizeChildNetworkData" , leader sends child  update request to SED instead of data response (refer to Issue #2107 "[mle] enable retransmission of network data to SEDs"). So the expected packet “data response” is not found and the case failed.

issue_title: MLE: Link-Local RLOC" address
issue_body:
I see that mle.cpp defines mLinkLocal16 address, that is registered to the interface when device is a SED (mle.cpp:519). 

What is a use of such link-local address? Thread device configures link-local address only based on its Extended MAC address.

Thread specification explicitly says: "Note that Thread devices do not configure a link-local address based on their 16-bit MAC address."

issue_title: convert.tdt()
issue_body:
**What command was run:**
in the _PmagPy_nb notebook:  
convert.tdt('../measurement_import/tdt_magic/')


**What happened:**
All files successfully created, but method_codes are not correct resulting in bizarre thellier_gui plots (see attached).  



**What I expected to happen:**
The problem seems to be in correctly identifying the treatment steps labeled XXX.4 in the file.  These are "additivity checks".  They are being given the code LT-PTRM-I but aren't that.  We need a new method code for this:  LT-PTRM-ADD.   This step is necessary for calculating the statistic dac. issue_title: Redirect std::cout
issue_body:
I'm interested in redirecting `std::cout` for my functions (some of which are long running library functions that report on progress). Is there an officially sanctioned way to redirect `std::cout` to python's stdout? (other than the custom `pybind11::print` function)? Running inside of a Jupyter notebook, for example, requires the output go to Python's stdout to be visible. 

After lots of googling and some playing around, I've come up with the following solution, which works well. I can make into a PR if this is useful and not already available. It's at https://github.com/GooFit/GooFit/blob/573044ea20eedc8444f38cc6a7ce66bef27d2524/python/Print.cpp .

issue_title: Ability to use pykube from inside a container running in Kubernetes
issue_body:
If you get a shell in a container that is currently running inside a kubernetes cluster, and install kubectl, you can access the Kubernetes cluster from the inside. There is however no kubeconfig file used, yet still kubectl manages to contact the master node and create API objects. The only thing exposed on the environment I found was:

```bash
root@my-busybox-lc4bi:~# env | grep 'KUBERNETES'
KUBERNETES_PORT=tcp://10.100.0.1:443
KUBERNETES_PORT_443_TCP_PORT=443
KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT=443
KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST=10.100.0.1
KUBERNETES_PORT_443_TCP_PROTO=tcp
KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT_HTTPS=443
KUBERNETES_PORT_443_TCP_ADDR=10.100.0.1
KUBERNETES_PORT_443_TCP=tcp://10.100.0.1:443
```

so I presume that kubectl must be using something like this to contact the master, but I'm not sure. It would be great if pykube could be able to contact the Kubernetes master from inside a container running in the cluster in the same way as kubectl manages to do without a kubeconfig file. We need this functionality for our main use case with the Galaxy workflow environment. I would be happy to contribute to this with some guidance, as I don't see very clearly how to approach this.

issue_title: acme API changes required for implementing the latest ACME spec
issue_labels: [{'id': 277074172, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20acme', 'name': 'area: acme', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20code%20health', 'name': 'area: code health', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 146954792, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/question', 'name': 'question', 'color': 'cc3399', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
In order to implement the current ACME spec, we're going to need to implement some breaking changes. Ideally I'd like to implement the new Python APIs on top of Boulder's old ACME version and offer the old APIs for a deprecation period while Boulder is still implementing the latest ACME spec. Once Boulder has implemented the latest spec, the deprecated `acme` module APIs would be supported only when using the old Boulder URLs. To use the new Boulder URLs, you would need to switch over to the new `acme` APIs. Then, at some point N months after deployment of the new Boulder URLs, the old Boulder URLs would stop working, and everyone using the `acme` module would need to be on the latest version by then and using the new APIs.

Alternately, we could make a hard breaking change, and say that anyone using `acme` and needing the old APIs should pin to an older version. Programs that are updated to use the newer `acme` APIs would pin a minimum `acme` version needed.

Here are the main things I can think of that need to change:

- Authz's are now created by the server as a result of submitting a CSR. We should add a client method `new_order(csr)`, which returns an Order object. The Order object will contain a number of authorization objects. Clients can fulfill those authorizations as normal. We would also add a new method `poll_for_issuance(order)` to be called once a client submitted answers to all the challenges.

- Registrations are renamed to Accounts, and JWS bodies are now identified by the Account URL rather than the full key. This means that when instantiating a Client, we need to include not just a key, but also an account URL. Probably the best way to do this is to allow constructing a Client with no key, and provide a `client.set_account(...)` method that would take an AccountResource that includes a URI (because it would inherit from ResourceWithURI).

- Terms of Service URL is now a property of the directory rather than the account. This means that we need a `terms-of-service-agreed` field in NewRegistration, and `client.agree_to_tos(account)` will become a no-op for new-style ACME. Clients will need to show the ToS from the directory before calling client.register.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Always save server value in renewal config
issue_body: Currently, if people use configuration files from 0.5.0+ and then use them with a version before 0.5.0, renewal fails because it expects the server value to be there. We should always save the server value in the renewal config to help mitigate this problem.

issue_title: NGINX option need a plug-and-play solution for Weak-DH bug
issue_body:
See all details at [this letsencrypt.org discussion](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/certbot-on-nginx-not-working-no-plug-and-play-solution/33895/6).

PROBLEM: the default ([see ubuntuxenial-nginx recommendation](https://certbot.eff.org/#ubuntuxenial-nginx)) of the most popular Linux is not working, because generates a  [Weak-DH "bug"](https://weakdh.org/).

WORKAROUND: a terminal sequence of commands... not is a real solution.

SOLUTION: the `certbot --nginx`  command must detect the problem (eg. by checking `/etc/nginx/ssl/` folder existence)  and run internally the "workaround instructions".

issue_title: certbot-auto renew --dry-run is starting apache and failing certificate renewal
issue_body:
When I try to run a dry run of my certificate renewal process, I understand that since apache2 is listening on port 80, I have to stop it. However, when I do stop it, as soon as certbot "installs" the first certificate, apache2 is started automatically. So the first certificate finishes with a success, and then the subsequent certificates fail because apache is listening on port 80. Rather annoying actually as no matter how many times I stop apache2, certbot (for some odd reason) re-enables it after checking the first cert. Any ideas of what is going on? I am running Ubuntu 14.04 trusty, and naturally my webserver is Apache.

Thanks in advance

issue_title: Don't block during startup while waiting for block to be mined
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Raiden can be quite slow when starting up since it needs to wait for a new block to be mined. This process is currently blocking, which is why it can take a long time to start raiden.

## Solution

Make the process of waiting for a block to be mined so that the node gets registered in the endpoint registry non-blocking

issue_title: Remove block numbers from the smart contract events
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

[NettingChannel](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b70cd3d5074ed4605b3a643834b74dc5c5604ed7/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelContract.sol#L9-L12) expose a few events with the block number, that value is duplicated since it's available through the [eth_getFilterChanges](https://github.com/ethereum/wiki/wiki/JSON-RPC#eth_getfilterchanges)

> blockNumber: QUANTITY - the block number where this log was in. null when its pending. null when its pending log.

## Solution

Remove the `block_number` fields from all smart contract event and use the result from the JSON RPC call.

## Tasklist

- [x] remove the block_number
  - [x] ChannelNewBalance
  - [x] ChannelClosed
  - [x] TransferUpdated
  - [x] ChannelSettled
- [x] Expose the `blockNumber` from the log to the [filter changes](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b70cd3d5074ed4605b3a643834b74dc5c5604ed7/raiden/network/rpc/client.py#L332-L357)
- [x] Fix the code that needs the block number.

issue_title: Asset Exchange Transfers
Recover from the Write Ahead Log
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

After having the Write Ahead Log written to we also need to be able to recover if Raiden restarts by reading and replaying all state changes from the log.

Also Write Tests.
## Solution

Read from the Write Ahead Log when raiden starts.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Write tests (xfailed) that specify the expected behaviour of a WAL recovery in both the happy path and situations where something changed in between.
- [ ] Read from the Write Ahead Log when Raiden starts and replay state changes to get to the latest state.

issue_title: Add an identifier field for Raiden's transfers
issue_body:
Add an identifier to raiden messages.
- The initiator of a Mediated Transfer cannot reuse a secret when a timeout happend, it needs to discard the current secret/hashlock and make a new transfer, as a consequence the hashlock cannot be used for identifying a transfer.
- Allow multiple channels to be used for a single transfer. When a single channel doesnt have enough funds to complete a transfer but multiple channels aggregate could sum up to desired transfer value, a unique identifier is required as an identifier.

TODO:
- [x] Decide on a identifier size (16/32/64 bits)
- [x] Add the identifier to messages:
  - [x] DirectTransfer
  - [x] MediatedTransfer
  - [x] SecretRequest

  > these changes need to be done in the files `raiden/messages.py`, `raiden/encoding/messages.py`, and the `raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol`

- [x] Add an identifier argument to the end-user-apis that wll be used with the messages
  - [x] raiden/console.py:Tools
  - [x] raiden/raiden_service.py:RaidenAPI

issue_title: Too many task files slows down SoS
issue_body:
This is separated from #796. There is slowness not only in job preparation but also at the end of every for loop in tasks -- my task queue will always halt for about 10 seconds before submitting the next batch of jobs, ie, the overhead of submitting 1000 jobs is 500 seconds. Today I finally can no longer live with the slowness so I removed my `~/.sos/tasks`. Out of curiosity I `tar zcf` before I delete it. It took quite a while to archive it, and generated for me a 1.9G `tar.gz` file. That might be a 10GB sqlite database if that's what we will end up doing, and growing ... that sounds disturbing.

This may be an important issue for heavy duty work. I'm really curious how other software deal with this problem.

issue_title: SoS goes to single core at multiple dependent steps
issue_body:
Sorry to keep posting here issues running large number of small jobs. I initially thought this is related to #971 but looks it is not. Basically I have a workflow like #971, but now I have 4 steps `A`, `B`, `C` and `D`. The complete sequences should be:

```
A -> B -> C
A -> B -> D
```

and I made it such that the default step depends on `C` and `D` which then depend on `A` and `B` respectively. In execution, I expect `A` and `B` be executed sequentially, then `C` and `D` in parallel. This should be a very intuitive outcome-oriented logic. 


But it was not the case. I do see `C` and `D` gets started the same time, but only `C` gets executed in parallel. `D` drags along with only one thread, until it finishes all the computation. This is weird.  I've got a MWE here:

[16-May-2018-drag.zip](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/files/2009342/16-May-2018-drag.zip)

This example has a slightly none-trivial step `A` but the rest steps are trivial. 

To reproduce the drag, first open `top` in another terminal, then

```
time sos run step_drag.sos -j 20
```

you'll see all seems fine (nicely parallelled) until steps `score_pi0_0` and `score_beta_0` (my `C` and `D`) above. One of them finished quickly but the other was dragging it running single process computation, although I would expect by logic that `C` and `D` should complete at the same time! The time it took was:

```
real	1m22.695s
```

Now, run the other example that removes step `D` only keep step `C`, 

```
time sos run step_no_drag.sos -s force -j 20
```

everything is paralleled and time took is only:

```
real	0m21.712s
```

So something strange is happening with this outcome-oriented style execution. The DAG file [drag_test.pdf](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/files/2009322/drag_test.pdf)
 looks good.  File lock is my only guess now. In my MWE zip file I also put in a version called `step_drag_task_version.sos` that removes all `concurrent = True` and replace with `task: ` for actions. Running that version, it seems `C` and `D` completed at the same time!

issue_title: Return path from path + str, allow path + path.
issue_body:
`path.py` treats `path` as string so:

```
str = path + str
str = str + path 
```

existing behavior:
```
str = path + str
```
There is no `str + path`, and `str += path` would fail. The tests work because they happen to have the form of `str += path + str`.

Proposed change:
```
path = path + str
```
which is similar to

```
path = path / str
```
and would allow

```
path + str + str + path
```

issue_title: separate command for the generation of workflow reports.
issue_body:
We generate workflow reports with option `-p` using runtime information saved in `.sos/workflow_signatures.db`. There is currently no way to generate the reports after the completion of workflow. It would be useful to allow users to browse through this database and view or generate reports after the completion of the workflows. Something like

```
sos show workflows
sos show workflow ID
```

Hi,

Thanks!

issue_title: Is there something wrong in normalization
issue_body:
```python
from torchvision import transforms
import torchvision.transforms.functional as F
import torch

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Normalize definition in torchvision.transforms.transforms.py
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
class Normalize(object):
    """Normalize an tensor image with mean and standard deviation.
    Given mean: ``(M1,...,Mn)`` and std: ``(S1,..,Sn)`` for ``n`` channels, this transform
    will normalize each channel of the input ``torch.*Tensor`` i.e.
    ``input[channel] = (input[channel] - mean[channel]) / std[channel]``
    Args:
        mean (sequence): Sequence of means for each channel.
        std (sequence): Sequence of standard deviations for each channel.
    """

    def __init__(self, mean, std):
        self.mean = mean
        self.std = std

    def __call__(self, tensor):
        """
        Args:
            tensor (Tensor): Tensor image of size (C, H, W) to be normalized.
        Returns:
            Tensor: Normalized Tensor image.
        """
        return my_normalize(tensor, self.mean, self.std)

    def __repr__(self):
        return self.__class__.__name__ + '(mean={0}, std={1})'.format(self.mean, self.std)

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Fundtion definition in torchvision.transforms.functional.py
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
def _is_tensor_image(img):
    return torch.is_tensor(img) and img.ndimension() == 3

def my_normalize(tensor, mean, std):
    """Normalize a tensor image with mean and standard deviation.
    See ``Normalize`` for more details.
    Args:
        tensor (Tensor): Tensor image of size (C, H, W) to be normalized.
        mean (sequence): Sequence of means for each channel.
        std (sequence): Sequence of standard deviations for each channely.
    Returns:
        Tensor: Normalized Tensor image.
    """
    if not _is_tensor_image(tensor):
        raise TypeError('tensor is not a torch image.')
    # TODO: make efficient
    result_tensor = []
    for t, m, s in zip(tensor, mean, std):
        result_tensor.append(t.sub_(m).div_(s))
    return torch.stack(result_tensor, 0)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Generate random image which range is (0 ~ 255)
    img = torch.rand(3, 160, 320) * 127.5 + 127.5

    # Define parameter I want to normalize
    my_mean = [127.5, 127.5, 127.5]
    my_std = [127.5, 127.5, 127.5]

    # Adopt official Normalization
    print("max value in tensor before adopt official: ", torch.max(img), img.size())
    norm_op = transforms.Normalize(my_mean, my_std)
    img = norm_op(img)
    print("max value in tensor after  adopt official: ", torch.max(img), img.size())

    # Adopt revision Normalization
    print("max value in tensor before adopt revision: ", torch.max(img), img.size())
    norm_op = Normalize(my_mean, my_std)
    img = norm_op(img)
    print("max value in tensor after  adopt revision: ", torch.max(img), img.size())
```
Sorry to bother.
Today I try to use normalization function to normalize my data.
However, I cannot get the right result eventually. 
As the result, I do the experiment.
The above code shows that I use official `transforms.Normalize` to deal with the generated data.
The following shows the result:
```
max value in tensor before adopt official:  254.99990844726562 torch.Size([3, 160, 320])
max value in tensor after  adopt official:  0.9999992847442627 torch.Size([3, 160, 320])
max value in tensor before adopt revision:  0.9999992847442627 torch.Size([3, 160, 320])
max value in tensor after  adopt revision:  -0.9921568632125854 torch.Size([3, 160, 320])
```
However, after the conduction of the function, the max value of tensor didn't change.
Next, I try to revise as the above edition, and get the right answer finally.
Is there something wrong with my idea?
Or is there some problem in official version?
The version of torchvision I use is 0.2.0

issue_title: Repetitive documentation of the raised exceptions
issue_body:
Several issues:
1. We wrapper some exceptions into zhmcclient exceptions, but loose information. That is the case for:
   - AuthError - we create that for any HTTP status 403, and loose the details returned by the HMC.
   
   Status: **Done** via PR #97.
   - ConnectionError - while we include the message of the underlying exception in the message of the ConnectionError, we loose details that could be machine-processible such as the socket errno number etc.
     
     Status: **Done** via PR #97.
2. We do not raise the following exceptions at all, currently:
   - ParseError
     
     Status: **Done** via PR #96.
   - VersionError
     
     Need to revisit whether we want VersionError to be raised. Discussion with Juergen: We may have the situation that a zhmcclient function depends on a minimum HMC version or HMC APi version, so that the exception could be used in the future. We keep the exception defined, for now. However, looking at the exception, it should be improved to capture the expected vs. available version, and which type of version the error is about (HMC API vs HMC itself).

     Status: **Done** via PR #110.
3. The zhmcclient methods mostly repeat the zhmcclient exceptions in their Raises: definition. Can we simplify that.
   
   Status: **Open** See **discussion point**, below.
4. The zhmcclient Python properties can raise exceptions but do not document that at all currently.
   
   Status: **Done** Verified that all Python properties that possibly can raise exceptions, document that.

issue_title: [Pandas on Ray] Cannot .loc by toDateTime Index
issue_body:
Hi @devin-petersohn , I retried my example on the new machine @pcmoritz gave me, and I ran into an issue where I could not run .loc by index after I converted the index to DateTime. The same code works in Pandas.

This time, I did figure how to print out the version of Ray that is installed on the new machine, so I hope you can let me know if this is indeed an old version of Ray or if this is a legitimate bug.

### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Ubuntu 16.04.4 LTS (Xenial Xerus)
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: Unknown. Please ask Philipp Moritz, he was the one who installed Ray for me.
- **Ray version**: 0.4.0
- **Python version**: Anaconda Python 3.6.4
- **Exact command to reproduce**: 
```
import ray.dataframe as pd
# import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("yellow_tripdata_2015-01-01.csv")
df = df.set_index('tpep_pickup_datetime')
df.index = pd.to_datetime(df.index)  # Ray does fine without this line

df.loc['2015-01-10 19:01:44']  #Error here
```


### Describe the problem
I get the traceback copied below in the **Source code/logs** section, when running the line `df.loc['2015-01-10 19:01:44']`. It complains that the Index is not unique-- indeed, there are multiple rows in the data that have the same timestamp for that column, and it is not monotonic either. 

However, in Pandas, this line normally returns a DataFrame of multiple rows that match the`2015-01-10 19:01:44` DateTime index. Ray correctly returns multiple rows if the index was not converted into DateTime first.

In case it helps, I'm under the impression this problem may have to do with the `pd.to_datetime` more than `DataFrame.loc` in this case. I once saw `pd.to_datetime` return all rows as `1970-01-01 00:00:00` when converting this column to DateTime (though I haven't reproduced that error since then), so I have seen funny behavior from `to_datetime` before from Pandas on Ray.

A similar error may have used to exist in an older version of Pandas, but was fixed in this Pandas Github issue: [https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/3659](url)

Also, this Pandas Github issue mentions in the reply chain that the correct behavior for .loc with non-unique indices is a DataFrame of matching rows: [https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/9466](url)

Additionally, I noticed when running Ray on this new machine that the memory errors seem **much** worse than before. In my original email example, I was using the January dataset (1/12 of all of 2015 data), and was able to run several parts in succession. 

However, this time when running Ray, it would hang and spam `ObjectID already exists in the object store` error messages immediately, on any next line, right after `pd.read_csv` (with my 1/12 data, running **isolated** from pandas or anything else). I wouldn't even be able to run `df.head()` right after reading in the csv this time around.

I had to split my January dataset in half, and use only 1/24 of the 2015 data, in order to just run this test. It may have just been that the new machine has less memory, but I do hope that I am indeed running from the right version of Ray.

You can get the source data csv I used at this page [http://www.nyc.gov/html/tlc/html/about/trip_record_data.shtml](url) under 2015>January>Yellow. Note that this csv file is 2 GB.

### Source code / logs
Traceback returned at `df.loc['2015-01-10 19:01:44']` line:

issue_title: [AWS Autoscaler] create_node hangs forever when insufficient AWS Capacity
issue_body:
In aws/node_provider.py:create_node, the call to ```self.ec2.create_instances``` can block indefinitely. This has happened to me for spot requests when AWS has no capacity, but it could plausibly happen in other situations too.

It might be more robust to use request_spot_instances, reserving create_instance only for on-demand instances; however, this would complicate the code significantly, as I think we'd need to poll for when the instances are created.

Alternately, we could switch to using {request,modify}_spot_fleet, which supports both on-demand and spot types. I think this is closest to how Amazon intends the API to be used, but it is a bit of a mismatch with the Ray autoscaling API. The main problem is in scaling down: I think we'd need to follow [this procedure](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45870613/terminate-specific-instance-in-aws-spot-fleet) to terminate a specific node. We'd also still need to poll to discover new nodes.

issue_body:
# Feature request

<!-- This template is for feature requests. Please fill out the following: -->


#### Select the type of feature you are requesting:

- [ ] Cog

#### Describe your requested feature

Feel free to describe in as much detail as you wish.

If you are requesting a cog to be included in core:

If you are requesting a command:

If you are requesting API functionality:
    - Describe what it should do
    - Note whether it is to extend existing functionality or introduce new functionality

Sentry is now used for logging errors of most Red instances, but only some core developers have access to it (Kowlin and orels1). I think we should make it so approved cog creators can access the errors from their cogs, so they can fix the errors quickly, and also have some statistics, like the number of instances using their cogs...

This could be done with a clear versionning system for repos (instead of being based on the latest commit, why not using the Github release system or give the version number in the `info.json` file?)

issue_title: Renaming myexe.link
issue_body:
I think we should have a general dependency name for one target on another. An executable might depend on a library. But an executable might also depend on a header only target! The build-system should just know what to do:

dependence on header only library:
  - Add include files

dependence on library:
  - Add include files
  - Add linking

so how about

```toml
# Build a library
[mylib]
target_type = "sharedlibrary"
[mylib.sources]
include_directories = ["mylib/include"]
source_directories = ["mylib/src"]

[myheaderlib]
target_type = "headerlibrary"
[myheaderlib.sources]
include_directories = ["myheaderlib/include"]

# Build an executable and link the library
[myexe]
output_name = "runExe"
target_type = "executable"
dependencies = ["mylib", "myheaderlib"]
[myexe.sources]
include_directories = ["myexe/include"]
source_directories = ["myexe/src"]
[myexe.flags]
link = ["-DMYLIB_SOME_DEFINE"]
```

issue_title: repo doesn't have URL (linking to docs, ideally)
Arbitrary kevent support
issue_body:
This is part of the long term plan to implement subprocess suport as outlined in https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/4#issuecomment-398967572 (see "Step 3", copied/pasted below for convenience).

---

On MacOS and the BSDs, there's this neat thing called "kqueue", which lets you efficiently wait on all kinds of different events in an async-event-loop-friendly way. Currently Trio just uses this to wait for file descriptors to become readable/writable, but in general kqueue is much more flexible. For example, the `EVFILT_PROC` event type lets you wait for a subprocess to exit.

`trio/_core/_io_kqueue.py` should provide the ability to wait on arbitrary event types. (Then we'll use that in our subprocess support to wait for `EVFILT_PROC` events.)

There's already a stub implementation of this, in the `monitor_kevent` method. This isn't tested or used currently though, so if we're going to start using it we'd want to make sure it actually works. (And possibly change how it works, if there's some other semantics that make it easier to use.)

issue_title: tests may failing if user has default git hooks installed
issue_body:
If you have any hooks inside `~/.git-template/` you may be surprised to see that `rdopkg` tests are failing. This is happening because during the testing some git repos are checkout and commits are made into them, using the default git settings of the current user.

The workaround for this is to tell git not to use the hooks during testing.

issue_title: PyCapnp or gRPC?
issue_body:
Hi,

First I'd like to say that I've been having lots of fun lately learning how to use pycapnp. I implemented a basic chat system (https://github.com/Cheaterman/capnchat) and it works and is cool.

However the main reason I was studying capnproto is to make a networking stack for multiplayer games. As far as I can see, there's no way to get pycapnp working with UDP at this moment (capnp itself only added UDP to KJ in 0.6, I suppose these functions aren't even wrapped, so not implemented in `TwoPartyServer`) - at least not for more than a single client (because of the way passing sockets is implemented - it apparently used to apply to connection strings too).

So, considering it doesn't have what I need, and the project itself seems very much abandoned, should I give up on pycapnp and use gRPC/protobuf instead?

Thanks!

issue_title: drain option...
issue_body:
Some times, when queues build up, you want to make a change to how the queues are defined.
To start fresh, one needs to create new queues with new bindings, but just doing a restart
will abandon the old queue. so would like to have an option to invoke the tools with the 'drain' argument. This will remove the queue bindings, so that the queue stops filling up, and can be drained.... so that no notifications are missed.

sr_subscribe --drain config start

wait until the queue is empty.

then stop it.
then change the config, and start without --drain, and will work normally.
.
.
.
with save/restore implemented. one could stop a consumer, note the queue (old_queue)
start a using a new queue (and new bindings.) Then start a shovel with -save -queue old_queue.
to obtain the messages from the old queue. 
sr_shovel -restore_queue new_queue 
will transfer the missing notifications to the new queue.
.
.
stopping the old queue, really means removing the binding on the old queue so that it stops getting messages.

 ... was: https://sourceforge.net/p/metpx/feature-requests/44/

I think most analysts would do this today using the rabbitmq management GUI which has substantially improved over the years.

issue_title: split socket.pyx into pyx/py
issue_body:
Only the basic methods of the socket need to be Cython:

* init
* close
* send
* recv
* getsockopt
* setsockopt

All the derivative and sugar methods (multipart, json, sockopts as attrs, etc.) do not need to be Cython, and do not benefit from being compiled.  I would like to split the Socket file into two: a minimal base class in Cython, and the extended methods on a subclass in Python.

This would reduce compile time, and significantly reduce the need to recompile when working on new features, as the core methods haven't been touched in quite some time.
